summary: Deep adversarial correctness audit of the FileID Windows Rust engine (15 subsystems, refute-by-default verify)
logs: Engine audit: 25 raw findings; 18 CONFIRMED (>=2/3 real); 5 contested (1 real vote)

confirmed.title: HNSW index built with an entropy-seeded RNG makes face clustering (and consolidation) nondeterministic on large libraries
confirmed.description: build() calls `Builder::default().build(embeds, values)` with no `.seed(...)`. In instant-distance 0.6.1, `Builder::default()` sets `seed: rand::random()` (a fresh getrandom-derived value per call), and that seed drives the per-point layer-assignment shuffle that determines the HNSW graph topology and therefore which approximate kNN neighbors each query returns. Both face-clustering HNSW call sites use this builder: face_clustering::cluster() at line 106 (HNSW path taken when faces.len() >= HNSW_MIN = 5000) and edges_hnsw() at line 262 (taken when cids.len() > HNSW_CONSOLIDATE_THRESHOLD = 2000 centroids). On a library above 5000 faces, Pass-1's kNN neighbor sets differ run-to-run, which changes connected components, hence cluster membership AND the 1-based cluster IDs, on every re-cluster. handle_run_face_clustering auto-fires after every scan and re-derives each cluster's inherited name by majority vote keyed on cluster_id (commands/face_clustering.rs lines 300-358), so People-tab identities and names can hop to different groups on an incremental rescan that added a single photo. The same nondeterminism affects which centroid merge edges edges_hnsw() proposes above 2000 clusters. This directly violates the documented determinism invariant the code claims to uphold (identity_clustering.rs lines 199-202; the `clustering_is_deterministic` proptest in face_clustering.rs lines 594-608). The test never catches it because proptest only runs count in 2..15, which is always the brute-force path (HNSW_MIN=5000), and hnsw_and_brute_edge_paths_agree uses orthogonal families where approximation cannot flip the union-find result.
confirmed.trigger: Scan a library with >5000 detected faces (or any state with >2000 face clusters) and run/auto-run face clustering twice on the identical DB. The two runs produce different cluster IDs / membership (and can re-attach saved names to different People cards), instead of the stable IDs the design promises.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the index deterministic by fixing the seed in hnsw_index::build, e.g. `Builder::default().seed(0xF11E1D).build(embeds, values)` (any fixed u64). This restores run-to-run reproducibility for both the cluster() Pass-1 kNN path and the edges_hnsw() consolidation path while keeping the HNSW perf win. Optionally add a >5000-face determinism test to guard the HNSW branch.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL after reading source, callers, callees, and the actual instant-distance 0.6.1 crate source.

(1) Seed claim confirmed in the crate itself: Cargo.lock pins instant-distance 0.6.1 (checksum 8c619cda...). In that crate's src/lib.rs, `impl Default for Builder` sets `seed: rand::random()` (line 108) — a fresh getrandom-derived value per call. `Hnsw::new` does `SmallRng::seed_from_u64(builder.seed)` (line 214) and then, at lines 257-270, assigns each point a random sort key via `rng.gen_range(0..n)` and `sort_unstable()`s on it. The crate's own comment (lines 252-254) states this deliberately randomizes each point's layer AND insertion order. So a different seed yields a different HNSW graph topology and therefore different approximate-kNN neighbor sets per query. The finder's central premise is exact.

(2) Call sites confirmed. hnsw_index::build (util/hnsw_index.rs:37) calls `Builder::default().build(embeds, values)` with no `.seed(...)`. face_clustering::cluster builds this index when `embeddings.len() >= HNSW_MIN (5_000)` (lines 97-107) and serves Pass-1 kNN from it (lines 112-125). edges_hnsw (lines 255-284) builds it unconditionally and is taken from consolidate() when `cids.len() > HNSW_CONSOLIDATE_THRESHOLD (2_000)` (line 359-363).

(3) Harm chain confirmed. identity_clustering Pass-1 (lines 182-194) does single-linkage `uf.union(i, hit.idx)` over each kNN hit ≥ pass1_cosine. Single-linkage is hypersensitive: one added/dropped bridge edge merges or splits whole connected components, changing membership and the 1-based cluster IDs remapped in face_clustering.rs:155-172. commands/face_clustering.rs:300-358 inherits prior person names by majority vote keyed on cluster_id, so shuffled membership/IDs re-attach saved People-tab names to different cards. This directly violates the determinism invariant the code documents (identity_clustering.rs:199-202) and asserts (clustering_is_deterministic proptest, face_clustering.rs:594-608).

(4) Why the test misses it, confirmed: the proptest uses `count in 2usize..15`, always below HNSW_MIN=5000, so it only exercises the brute-force path; the HNSW branch's determinism is never guarded. hnsw_and_brute_edge_paths_agree uses orthogonal one-hot families where cross-family cosine ~0 and within-family ~0.999, so approximation cannot flip union-find — it tests correctness, not run-to-run stability.

(5) Suggested fix is valid: `pub fn seed(mut self, seed: u64) -> Self` exists at lib.rs:65 and chains before .build(); `Builder::default().seed(0xF11E1D).build(...)` compiles and pins the topology, restoring determinism for both paths while keeping the perf win.

Caveats lowering confidence from certainty: (a) I could not execute a >5000-face two-run diff on hardware (the env's runtime/GPU corpus path is owner-gated), so the membership-flip is established by logic, not by a live repro — but the seed entropy + single-linkage sensitivity make a flip overwhelmingly likely on real noisy SFace embeddings, where many genuine neighbors sit near the threshold. (b) lib.rs:317 also builds layers via rayon into_par_iter, an independent potential nondeterminism source; fixing the seed addresses the primary, claimed cause but a fully reproducible build may also need to confirm the parallel insertion is order-insensitive. Neither caveat refutes the finding: the entropy seed alone makes the documented determinism guarantee false on any library above the HNSW thresholds.

Verified against actual source in both the repo and the pinned instant-distance 0.6.1 crate.

UPSTREAM MECHANISM (instant-distance 0.6.1, ~/.cargo/.../instant-distance-0.6.1/src/lib.rs):
- lib.rs:102-112 `impl Default for Builder` sets `seed: rand::random()` — a fresh getrandom-derived u64 per call. (The claim is exactly right.)
- lib.rs:214 `let mut rng = SmallRng::seed_from_u64(builder.seed);` then lines 257-260 `shuffled = (0..len).map(|i| (PointId(rng.gen_range(0..len)), i)).collect(); shuffled.sort_unstable();` — the seed drives the per-point key that fixes insertion order and layer assignment, i.e. the HNSW graph topology, hence which approximate kNN each query returns. Confirmed.

REPO SITE (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/util/hnsw_index.rs:37): `Builder::default().build(embeds, values)` with no `.seed(...)`. Both call sites use it: face_clustering.rs:106 (HNSW when faces.len() >= HNSW_MIN=5000, lines 97-107) and edges_hnsw at line 262 (HNSW when cids.len() > HNSW_CONSOLIDATE_THRESHOLD=2000, lines 232/359-363).

NEIGHBORS FEED UNION-FIND DIRECTLY (identity_clustering.rs:182-194): `for hit in searcher(i) { ... uf.union(i, hit.idx); }` — Pass-1 connected components are derived straight from the HNSW neighbor sets, with no exact re-scoring on this path. Different approximate neighbors near the pass1_cosine=0.66 boundary (k_nn=10) flip unions, changing component membership and the 1-based IDs assigned at face_clustering.rs:155-172.

HARM (commands/face_clustering.rs:300-358): inherited names are chosen by majority vote keyed on cluster_id, run after every auto-fired re-cluster, so shifting membership/IDs migrates saved People-tab names to different cards.

INVARIANT EXPLICITLY CLAIMED + TEST GAP CONFIRMED: identity_clustering.rs:199-202 documents deterministic IDs across runs; the clustering_is_deterministic proptest (face_clustering.rs:594-608) uses count in 2..15, always below HNSW_MIN=5000, so it never exercises the HNSW branch; hnsw_and_brute_edge_paths_agree (line 814) uses 12 orthogonal families (within-family cos ~0.999, cross ~0) where approximation cannot flip union-find. Neither guards the seed.

REACHABILITY LENS: The path is reachable — a 4 GB target library routinely exceeds 5000 faces, the handler auto-fires after every scan, and nothing upstream guards or fixes the seed. The output is non-reproducible BY CONSTRUCTION (entropy seed), directly violating the documented invariant the cluster-ID-keyed name inheritance relies on. Note edges_hnsw re-scores exact cosine and dedups (lines 276-279), so its EDGE WEIGHTS are exact, but the candidate SET it proposes still depends on the run-varying HNSW topology, so near-threshold merges can appear/disappear. Pass-1 has no such mitigation. Suggested fix (`.seed(<const>)`) is correct and trivial.

Confidence 0.88 (not higher) only because the practical magnitude of run-to-run divergence depends on HNSW recall at ef_search=100; in principle high recall could make divergence rare for well-separated identities. But the design promises bit-identical IDs and the code depends on it, so an entropy seed is a genuine correctness defect regardless of frequency.

CONFIRMED by reading source + the exact installed dependency.

(1) hnsw_index.rs:37 calls Builder::default().build(...) with no .seed(). I read the actual installed crate at ~/.cargo/registry/.../instant-distance-0.6.1/src/lib.rs: impl Default for Builder (lines 101-108) sets `seed: rand::random()` — a fresh entropy value per call. No seed override exists anywhere in the engine (grep found only an unrelated getrandom in hmac.rs; Cargo.toml pins instant-distance = "0.6", resolved to 0.6.1).

(2) That seed drives topology: lib.rs:214 `SmallRng::seed_from_u64(builder.seed)`, then lines 257-259 `rng.gen_range(0..points.len())` assigns each point a random sort key that fixes insertion order / layer placement. Different seed => different HNSW graph => different approximate-kNN sets for the same query. So build() is nondeterministic exactly as claimed.

(3) Both call sites reach the HNSW path: cluster() Pass-1 at face_clustering.rs:100-106 when faces>=HNSW_MIN(5000); edges_hnsw() at line 262, taken at lines 359-362 when cids>HNSW_CONSOLIDATE_THRESHOLD(2000).

(4) Harm is real on the cluster() path: identity_clustering.rs:182-193 unions i with every kNN hit above pass1_cosine, and the approximate kNN set IS the edge set (no exact re-check). With k_nn=10, pass1_cosine=0.66 (identity_clustering.rs:73,79), a true neighbor near 0.66 that HNSW returns in run A but misses in run B flips a single-linkage union, changing connected components -> cluster membership -> the 1-based IDs assigned in first-seen order (face_clustering.rs:155-172). Name inheritance is a majority vote over which faces land in each cluster (commands/face_clustering.rs:300-335), so a membership shuffle can re-attach saved People names to different cards. This directly violates the documented determinism invariant at identity_clustering.rs:199-202.

(5) Triggered routinely, not just manually: AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync() fires from EngineClient.Apply (EngineClient.cs:1066,1104) after pipeline events, so an incremental rescan re-clusters automatically.

(6) Tests genuinely miss it: clustering_is_deterministic uses count in 2..15 (face_clustering.rs:595) — always below HNSW_MIN=5000, always the brute path; hnsw_and_brute_edge_paths_agree (lines 813-858) uses orthogonal one-hot families (cross-family cosine ~0, within ~0.999) where approximation cannot flip union-find, per its own fixture comment.

Nuance held back from full certainty: the edges_hnsw()/consolidate path is milder — HNSW only PROPOSES candidates and re-scores with EXACT cosine (lines 268-280), with strongest-edge-first union-find, so missing a very-high-cosine duplicate is less likely there; harm on that path is bounded to borderline merges. But the cluster() Pass-1 path has no exact re-check and is genuinely nondeterministic and harmful at >=5000 faces, which is a realistic library size for this product. The suggested fix (fixing the seed in hnsw_index::build) is correct and minimal. Bug is genuine, reachable, and harmful.

Key files: platforms/windows/src/engine/src/util/hnsw_index.rs:37; platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/face_clustering.rs:100-106,155-172,255-284,594-608,813-858; platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/identity_clustering.rs:73-79,182-202; platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/face_clustering.rs:236-266,300-335; C:/Users/adamm/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/instant-distance-0.6.1/src/lib.rs:101-108,214,257-259.

confirmed.title: Mid-pass GPU device-removal leaves an image row failed=false, stranding it permanently in the incremental skip-set
confirmed.description: The top-of-function GPU-dead guard (line 1289) is the ONLY place that sets tagged.failed=true for image/video. But when DXGI_ERROR_DEVICE_REMOVED is detected DURING this file's GPU stages (SFace embed at 1413-1419, SCRFD detect at 1427-1430, MobileCLIP at 1466/1487, or RAM++ at 1584-1587), the handler only calls coord.mark_gpu_dead() and break/continue — it never sets tagged.failed=true. Execution falls through: gpu_alive becomes false, the face block (1352), CLIP block (1451), RAM++ block (1510) and OCR (1668) are all skipped, and at line 1726 tags_evaluated is set to false. The row is emitted with failed=false, faces empty, clip_embedding=None, tags empty, plus a fresh scanned_unix. mark_gpu_dead() also flips cancelled=true, and the dbwriter explicitly flushes in-flight rows on cancel (dbwriter.rs:136-147), so this stranded row is COMMITTED to disk. The incremental skip-set is `failed = 0 AND scanned_at >= modified_at` (scan_session.rs:200-205), so this fully-empty image is recorded as scanned-and-fine and is skipped FOREVER on every later incremental scan — it never gets faces, embedding, or tags even after the GPU recovers on a fresh engine launch. This is exactly the 'stranded permanently' trap the code guards against elsewhere (scan.rs:216-263, the top-of-fn guard at 1289-1305).
confirmed.trigger: Run a scan; a GPU TDR / device-removed fires while a worker is mid-inference on an image (the common moment for TDR — heavy sustained GPU load). That worker, plus every other worker whose image future is already past the line-1289 check at that instant (up to worker_count ~9-14 in-flight images), each emit a failed=false row with all ML fields empty. The dbwriter cancel-drain commits them. On the next incremental scan those images are silently skipped and stay face-less / tag-less / un-embedded.
confirmed.suggested_fix: On the mid-pass device-removed branches (and/or right before the final return), mark image/video rows failed when the GPU died during processing: e.g. at line 1726 add `if coord.is_gpu_dead() && matches!(file.kind, FileKind::Image | FileKind::Video) { tagged.failed = true; tagged.error_message.get_or_insert_with(|| "GPU device removed mid-scan; file not fully processed (will retry next scan)".into()); }` so the failed=1 row forces a re-process, mirroring the top-of-function guard's contract.

verdicts.reasoning:
Independently re-derived the full causal chain in platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/tagging.rs (process_file_predecoded) and corroborating files; the bug is genuine, triggerable, and harmful.

1) Only-two-setters fact confirmed: `tagged.failed = true` appears ONLY at line 1297 (top-of-fn GPU-dead guard) and line 1316 (image-decode failure). `tagged.failed` is initialized false at line 1208. The mid-pass device-removed handlers (SFace 1413-1419, SCRFD 1426-1430, MobileCLIP 1465-1473 / 1486-1494, RAM++ 1583-1591, and BGE 1256-1259) only call `coord.mark_gpu_dead()` and break/fall through — none touch `tagged.failed`.

2) mark_gpu_dead (coordinator.rs:56-62) sets gpu_dead=true AND cancelled=true. gpu_dead is intentionally never reset (coordinator.rs:91-99), so recovery requires a full restart — exactly the moment an incremental rescan runs.

3) Fall-through path: a worker passes the 1289 guard (GPU alive), decodes successfully (so 1316 doesn't fire), then the GPU dies during a GPU stage. The later blocks gate on live `!coord.is_gpu_dead()` (1451, 1510, 1668) so CLIP/RAM++/OCR are skipped, faces are empty/partial. At line 1726 `tags_evaluated = !is_gpu_dead() && !failed && ... = false`, but `tagged.failed` is STILL false. Row emitted with failed=false + fresh scanned_unix (1181-1184).

4) The stranded row IS committed: in the worker loop (tagging.rs:782-852) the cancel-check `coord.check()` is at line 783 BEFORE processing; there is NO cancel-check between the function return at 844 and the unconditional `tx.send(tagged)` at 845. mark_gpu_dead set cancelled=true, so the dbwriter cancel-drain (dbwriter.rs:136-147) flushes the in-flight buffer with no filtering. The INSERT binds failed=0 (line 312) and fresh scanned_at (line 302); ON CONFLICT(path_text) overwrites failed=excluded.failed (line 620) and scanned_at=excluded.scanned_at (line 611).

5) Permanent strand: the incremental skip-set is `failed = 0 AND scanned_at >= modified_at` (scan_session.rs:200-205). The stranded row (failed=0, scanned_at=now >= modified_at) is in the skip-set, so it is skipped forever on later incremental scans — face-less, embedding-less, tag-less — even after a fresh launch recovers the GPU.

Contract corroboration: the top-of-fn guard comment (1282-1288) and the model-load-abort logic (scan.rs:216-263) both explicitly describe this exact "failed=false + fresh scanned_at => stranded forever in the timestamp-based skip-set" trap and guard against it elsewhere. The mid-pass branches are the one gap. The suggested fix (set failed=true for Image/Video when is_gpu_dead() at the final return) correctly mirrors the 1297 contract.

Scoping/honesty: blast radius is bounded to the images in flight at the instant of the TDR (<= worker_count, capped at 6 on low-mem), and TDR itself is rare; for a file with prior good tags the tags are preserved (tags_evaluated=false skips the tag DELETE) though failed/scanned_at are still overwritten. The most acute loss is first-ever scans of new images during a TDR. None of this refutes the mechanism — it remains genuine silent data-loss with no user-visible error, matching the high severity. Confidence 0.9 rather than higher only because the exact runtime ordering of the dbwriter cancel-drain vs. the in-flight send (whether the send always lands in the buffer before the drain) depends on async scheduling, but the code path makes the stranded-commit clearly reachable.

Verified against source. The mid-pass device-removed handlers (tagging.rs SFace 1414-1419, SCRFD 1427-1430, MobileCLIP 1466-1469/1487-1490, RAM++ 1584-1587) all call coord.mark_gpu_dead() then break/fall-through and NEVER set tagged.failed. The only place that sets failed=true for image/video is the top-of-function PRE-stage guard (1289-1305) and image-decode failure (1316) — neither covers a GPU that dies DURING this file's stages. tagged.failed is initialized false (1208) and at the final return (1726) only tags_evaluated is computed (= !is_gpu_dead() && !failed → false); failed stays false.

Reachability of the cited path is solid: (1) The interrupted in-flight file already passed the worker's coord.check() at the top of the loop (tagging.rs:782-784), so process_file_predecoded runs to completion and returns a failed=false row. (2) That row is sent UNCONDITIONALLY via tx.send(tagged) at 845 — no GPU-dead/cancelled filter between the function return and the send. (3) mark_gpu_dead() (coordinator.rs:56-63) flips cancelled=true on the first caller. (4) The dbwriter's cancel arm (dbwriter.rs:136-147) explicitly flushes the in-flight buffer before breaking, and the INSERT writes failed = excluded.failed (=0) + scanned_at = excluded.scanned_at (fresh) (dbwriter.rs:584/611-619). So at least the row held by the worker that detected the death is committed with failed=0 and a fresh timestamp.

Downstream harm confirmed: the incremental skip-set is exactly `failed = 0 AND scanned_at >= modified_at` (scan_session.rs:200-201), built into the discovery skip set, so an untouched image (modified_at unchanged) is skipped on every later incremental scan and never re-acquires faces/embedding/tags even after the GPU recovers on restart. This is precisely the "stranded permanently / face scanning stays broken" trap the codebase documents guarding against (scan.rs:216-227, tagging.rs:1282-1305) — but those guards only cover the pre-stage case, not the mid-pass case. GPU TDR mid-inference under sustained load on the documented 4GB DirectML target is a real, satisfiable precondition (the whole gpu_dead subsystem exists for it).

Skeptical counter-checks rejected: tags_evaluated=false does not set failed (only gates tag replacement); the channel is not dropped before the detecting worker's send; an untouched file's modified_at never advances. Confidence is 0.9 rather than higher only because exact drain ordering for OTHER simultaneously-in-flight workers' rows is timing-dependent — but the detecting worker's own row is reliably committed, which alone makes the bug real and harmful.

Verified the full causal chain independently in the actual source.

CHAIN:
1. tagging.rs:1208 initializes tagged.failed=false; scanned_unix is set unconditionally at entry (1181-1184).
2. The top-of-function GPU-dead guard (1289-1305) is the ONLY place that sets tagged.failed=true for Image/Video. It runs ONCE near the top, BEFORE the per-file GPU stages.
3. The mid-pass device-removed handlers — SFace (1413-1419), SCRFD (1427-1430), MobileCLIP pool (1466) and batch (1487), RAM++ (1584-1587) — each only call coord.mark_gpu_dead() and break/fall through. NONE sets tagged.failed=true.
4. coordinator.rs:56-63: mark_gpu_dead() latches gpu_dead=true AND cancelled=true.
5. Fall-through stages (CLIP 1451, RAM++ 1510, OCR 1668) are gated on !is_gpu_dead() and are skipped. The 1289 guard does NOT re-run for this file.
6. Final line 1726 sets tags_evaluated=false (correct), but tagged.failed remains false. The row is returned.
7. Worker loop (tagging.rs:845) sends tagged to the dbwriter unmodified — no failed mutation.
8. dbwriter cancel-drain (dbwriter.rs:136-147) flushes the in-flight buffer and tx.commit() (488) makes it durable: f.failed as i64 → failed column (312), f.scanned_unix → scanned_at (302). No rollback on cancel.
9. Incremental skip-set (scan_session.rs:197-205): `failed = 0 AND scanned_at >= modified_at` → this fully-empty image (faces empty, clip_embedding None, tags empty) is skipped FOREVER on later incremental scans, even after a fresh engine launch restores the GPU (coordinator.rs:91 — gpu_dead never reset, requires restart).
10. No remediation exists: scan_session.rs:386-401 only emits a gpu_device_removed error + Failed phase; it does NOT reconcile/re-fail any committed file rows.

HARM (impact lens): On a FIRST scan of a never-tagged image, the row is freshly INSERTed with failed=0, all ML fields empty, fresh scanned_at — then permanently excluded from re-processing. This is real data-loss / permanent feature-omission, exactly the "stranded face-less permanently" trap the code explicitly documents and guards against at tagging.rs:1289-1305 and scan.rs:216-263. The tags_evaluated/faces_evaluated=false flags (respected at dbwriter.rs:345/456) correctly prevent WIPING prior data on a re-scan of an already-good file, so a previously-tagged file isn't blanked — but it is still re-stamped scanned-and-fine and won't be retried, and a first-time file gets nothing. Up to worker_count (~9-14) in-flight images can be affected per TDR event. The suggested fix (mark failed for Image/Video when is_gpu_dead at the final return) is correct in shape and mirrors the top-of-fn guard's contract.

Confidence 0.9 (not 1.0) because the worst harm requires the affected images to be first-time/untagged at the moment of TDR; previously-good files are degraded (re-stamped, never refreshed) rather than wiped. Either way the failed=0 + fresh-timestamp + skip-forever outcome is genuine, triggerable, and harmful.

confirmed.title: Mid-flight GPU death drops an audio file's metadata tags and strands the row (no FTS fallback for audio tags)
confirmed.description: audio_tags (artist/album/title/genre/year, extracted on the decoder thread) are appended to tagged.tags at line 1274, BEFORE the GPU-dead guard at line 1289. If the GPU was marked dead by another worker between this audio file entering process_file_predecoded and reaching line 1289, the guard takes the early-return path: for Audio, needed_gpu=false so tagged.failed stays false, and tags_evaluated is never set (remains false from init at line 1218). The dbwriter only persists source='auto' tags when tags_evaluated is true (dbwriter.rs:456), so the audio metadata tags are silently dropped. Unlike docs (whose text survives via doc_text/doc_fts, gated on doc_stage_ran), audio tags have NO alternate persistence path — they exist only as `tags`. The row commits failed=false with fresh scanned_at, so the incremental skip-set (failed=0 AND scanned_at>=modified_at) skips it forever; the audio file's artist/album/etc. are lost until a manual rescan.
confirmed.trigger: A GPU TDR / device-removed occurs (triggered by any image worker) while audio files are in-flight in other workers' futures, after their audio_tags were pushed (1274) but before/at line 1289. Each such audio row is committed failed=false with empty persisted tags and is then permanently skipped on incremental rescans.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat the GPU-dead early-return as 'this CPU-extracted work was not persisted' for rows that carry CPU-only tags: either set tagged.failed=true when the row carries un-persisted audio/keyword tags it can't otherwise recover, or set tagged.tags_evaluated=true on the early-return for Doc/Audio kinds whose tags were fully computed on the CPU before the guard (and were unaffected by the GPU death), so the dbwriter persists them.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full logic chain against the actual source and it holds.

DATA FLOW: Audio files flow through the decoder thread, which extracts audio metadata unconditionally via audio_meta::extract (tagging.rs:1004-1010), carried in PreDecoded.audio_tags. In process_file_predecoded these are pushed into tagged.tags at line 1274 BEFORE the GPU-dead guard, and unconditionally (the only gate is online_only, applied at extraction time).

THE GUARD (1289-1304): `if coord.is_gpu_dead()` computes `needed_gpu = matches!(file.kind, FileKind::Image | FileKind::Video)` (line 1296). For Audio this is false, so `tagged.failed = needed_gpu` = false (line 1297), then `return tagged` at line 1304. This early return bypasses line 1726 (`tagged.tags_evaluated = !coord.is_gpu_dead() && !tagged.failed && !file.online_only`), so tags_evaluated stays at its init value false (line 1218). The comment at 1290-1295 explicitly justifies leaving Audio failed=false — but it overlooks that the CPU-extracted audio tags it already pushed at 1274 are then never persisted.

PERSISTENCE GATE (dbwriter.rs:456): auto-tag insert is gated `if f.tags_evaluated`. With tags_evaluated=false, the tag DELETE+INSERT block is skipped entirely → the audio artist/album/title/genre/year tags are silently dropped. I confirmed dbwriter.rs has NO audio/artist/album columns (grep found none) and no audio-equivalent of doc_stage_ran/ocr_stage_ran — tags is the ONLY persistence path for audio metadata. Unlike docs (doc_text/doc_fts gated on doc_stage_ran set at line 1240 before the guard, which survives), audio has no fallback.

PERMANENT STRANDING: The row commits failed=false with a fresh scanned_unix (set 1181-1192). The incremental skip-set (scan_session.rs:200-201) selects `failed = 0 AND scanned_at >= modified_at`, so this row is added to skip_paths and never reprocessed on incremental rescans — only rescan=true (line 161) recovers it.

TRIGGER IS REAL: gpu_dead is a shared Arc<AtomicBool> (coordinator.rs:29), set one-shot by ANY worker via mark_gpu_dead (e.g. image-worker SCRFD/ArcFace device-removed at tagging.rs:1415/1428). worker_count tokio tasks run concurrently sharing the same coordinator (tagging.rs:764-772). The window is actually WIDER than the finder stated: any audio file reaching line 1289 after the flag flips (not just the 1274→1289 gap) hits this, since audio_tags are extracted on the decoder thread regardless of GPU state.

Minor inaccuracy (does not weaken the finding): doc keyword-tag chips (lines 1243-1244) are ALSO dropped via the same tags_evaluated gate, so the finder's 'docs fully survive' contrast is only partly right (doc TEXT survives, doc tag chips don't). Under the trigger/reachability lens this is reachable with real inputs and harmful — hence real.

- audio_tags are extracted on the decoder thread (tagging.rs:1001-1010, audio_meta::extract) and carried on PreDecoded.
- In process_file_predecoded, tagged.tags_evaluated is initialized false (line 1218).

CONCURRENCY/REACHABILITY:
- worker_count workers each tokio::spawn a loop sharing one cloned ScanCoordinator (tagging.rs:764-793). confirmed.title: Empty-folder / "already up to date" notice is racily dropped when discovery drains in <250 ms
confirmed.description:
The discovery `tick` task (spawned at scan_session.rs:257) is the SOLE emitter of the `empty_folder` and `rescan_no_changes` IPC error events (verified: grep shows scan_session.rs:291/302 are the only producers; the C# app consumes `rescan_no_changes` at EngineClient.Commands.cs:156). The tick task's very first statement is `tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(250)).await` (scan_session.rs:259) — it cannot emit anything until at least 250 ms after spawn. Its only chance to emit the empty/rescan notice is when it later observes `discovered_done == true` with `count == 0`.

Meanwhile, when Discovery produces zero files (empty folder, all-unsupported, or an incremental rescan where every enumerated file is in the skip set), Discovery sets `done=true` and drops `tx`, closing the channel. Tagging's pump (`input.recv()` returns None) closes its output, so DbWriter.run() immediately hits the `Ok(None)` arm (dbwriter.rs:120) with an empty buffer and returns `(0,0)`. `writer.run(...).await?` (scan_session.rs:351-363) therefore returns, and `tick.abort()` (scan_session.rs:368) fires — all of which can complete in well under 250 ms because no ML inference ever runs on the empty input. The tick task is aborted while still parked in its initial sleep and never emits the notice.

The code at scan_session.rs:365-368 explicitly anticipates that the tick may be aborted before observing `done` but dismisses it as benign 'belt-and-suspenders', not realizing the tick is the only thing that surfaces the empty/already-current explanation. With the notice suppressed, control falls through to the non-cancelled/non-gpu-dead else branch (scan_session.rs:405-419), so the user gets `PhaseChanged(Completed)` + `ScanComplete{total_files:0}` with no explanation instead of the intended `empty_folder` (#21) or `rescan_no_changes` message.
confirmed.trigger: Re-scan (rescan=false) a small, already-scanned folder (e.g. ~10 photos, all current → all in the skip set so count stays 0), OR start a scan on a tiny empty/all-unsupported folder. Discovery + tagging + DbWriter drain in <250 ms, `tick.abort()` runs before the tick's first sleep elapses, and the app receives `Completed`/`ScanComplete{0}` with no `rescan_no_changes` / `empty_folder` event — the 'Library is already up to date' / 'No supported files found' guidance is silently lost.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the empty/rescan/partial notice independent of the tick's sleep timing. Before `tick.abort()` (or in place of relying on the tick for this), after `writer.run(...)` returns, re-check `discovered_done` + `discovered_count` + `discovered_errors` and emit the appropriate `rescan_no_changes` / `empty_folder` / `discovery_partial` event directly if it wasn't already sent. Alternatively, have the tick emit on its first iteration without the leading 250 ms sleep (sleep at the end of the loop), or await the discovery `done` flag synchronously before aborting. Guard against double-emit with an AtomicBool the tick and the post-drain check both honor.

confirmed.title: Cancel can't interrupt an in-flight VLM server request (up to 300 s latency per call)
confirmed.description: On the persistent-server path the cooperative cancel flag is only polled BETWEEN server.complete() calls (lines 400, 409, 420). The complete() call itself (vlm_server.rs:176-183) is a plain reqwest POST awaited with no cancellation hook and the client's only bound is a 300 s overall timeout (vlm_server.rs:110). When the user clicks Cancel while a complete() is in flight, nothing aborts the request: it runs to completion (or to the 300 s timeout). In Both mode each file issues THREE sequential complete() calls, so a single file can ignore the cancel for the duration of an in-progress call. On the documented 4 GB DirectML target a 7B VLM spills layers to CPU and a single caption/tag/rename call legitimately takes tens of seconds, so 'Cancel' visibly does nothing for a long time, and a stalled server stretches that to the full 300 s. The per-file CLI path (vlm.rs:232-255) does poll cancel every 100 ms and kills the child, so the two backends have very different cancel behavior.
confirmed.trigger: Start Deep Analyze All / Folder on a real library, let the persistent llama-server start, then click Cancel while a file is mid-completion. The batch keeps running the current file's HTTP call(s) for up to 300 s before the next loop-top cancel check fires.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the in-flight request cancellable: wrap each server.complete().await in a `tokio::select!` against a cancel signal (e.g. spawn the request as a task and abort it, or race it against a `tokio::time::sleep`-polled `cancel` AtomicBool and drop the request future on cancel). Also give the reqwest client a much shorter per-request/connect timeout than 300 s so a wedged server can't pin cancel latency to five minutes.

confirmed.title: Server death mid-batch fails every remaining file with no liveness recheck or CLI fallback
confirmed.description: Once the persistent server passes the up-front self-test and `server` is Some, the per-file loop unconditionally routes every file through analyze_file_via_server (line 530-532). There is no per-file liveness check (no child.try_wait, no health re-probe) and no fallback to the per-file CLI even though `runner` is frequently Some at this point. If the llama-server process dies mid-batch — highly plausible on the 4 GB target where a 7B Q4 model + KV cache + a large image can trip the OS OOM killer, or where a specific image crashes the mmproj — every subsequent complete() returns a connection error, so each remaining file is recorded as failed (line 556-559) and the whole rest of the library is wasted. The known-good CLI backend that was already located is never used as a salvage path.
confirmed.trigger: Run Deep Analyze All on the 4 GB box with Qwen2.5-VL 7B; the server starts and self-test passes, then the server process is killed by the OS (low memory) or crashes on a particular file partway through. Every file after that point reports failed and the batch completes with a large `(N failed)` count despite a working llama-mtmd-cli.exe being installed.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Detect a dead server (e.g. surface the connection error distinctly from a per-file decode error, or probe /health) and, when `runner.is_some()`, transparently fall back to the per-file CLI for the remainder of the batch — or attempt one server restart — instead of silently failing every remaining file.
