In run_deep_analyze_batch (deep_analyze.rs), `server` is bound ONCE before the loop (lines 339-375: Some only after VlmServer::start + vlm_server_payload_ok self-test pass) and is never re-evaluated, restarted, or liveness-checked inside the per-file loop. The dispatch at lines 530-541 is `if let Some(srv)=server.as_ref() { analyze_file_via_server(...) } else if let Some(r)=runner.as_ref() { CLI } else { error }` — so while `server.is_some()`, every file goes through the server and the CLI `runner` (frequently Some, resolved at line 319) is never consulted as a fallback.

Dead-server propagation confirmed: VlmServer::complete (vlm_server.rs:145-188) issues `self.client.post(...).send().await.context("VLM chat/completions request")?`. If the OS kills the llama-server process mid-batch, this send() returns a connection error which becomes Err and propagates via `?` out of analyze_file_via_server (deep_analyze.rs:403/412/424). Back in the loop, the Err arm (lines 556-559) simply does `failed += 1` + a warn and continues — with NO distinction between a transport/connection error (dead server) and a per-file decode error. The next file routes to the same still-Some `server` (the VlmServer value/`_child` handle is intact; only the OS process died, so kill_on_drop at line 103 hasn't fired and server.as_ref() is still Some), failing identically. Result: every remaining file is recorded failed and the batch completes with a large false (N failed) count.

No liveness/restart facility exists: the only child.try_wait() is in start_with_binary (vlm_server.rs:120), used during startup, not the batch. Grep across the engine shows VlmServer exposes only start + complete and the batch loop is its sole consumer — no higher-level retry/health layer. The finder did not misread anything; the cited lines and mechanism are accurate.

Trigger is plausible on the documented 4 GB DirectML/NVIDIA target running a 7B Q4 VLM with -ngl 99 / -c 4096 plus large base64-inlined images (OOM-kill or per-image mmproj crash). Harm is real: an entire whole-library Deep Analyze pass is wasted after a single mid-batch death despite a working CLI runner being available.

Confidence 0.85 (not higher) because: (a) the exact frequency of an actual OS OOM-kill vs graceful behavior on this hardware is not directly observed in-repo, and (b) the VlmServer is killed/dropped on function return so a user re-run would restart it — the damage is bounded to the current pass — but that still matches the claimed medium severity (wasted remainder + misleading failed count, no salvage path).

Verified against the actual source. In run_deep_analyze_batch (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/deep_analyze.rs:271), server is bound once up-front (lines 339-375) and runner (the CLI VlmRunner) is also typically Some (lines 319-322; vlm_server.rs:13 notes the b9254 runtime ships BOTH llama-mtmd-cli.exe and llama-server.exe). The per-file dispatch at lines 530-541 checks `if let Some(srv) = server.as_ref()` every iteration, but server is immutable, so once it is Some EVERY file in the batch goes through analyze_file_via_server and the `else if let Some(r) = runner` CLI branch (533-534) is unreachable for the whole batch. There is no per-file liveness recheck: no child.try_wait, no /health re-probe in the loop. VlmServer holds the child only for kill_on_drop and explicitly "never reads it directly" (vlm_server.rs:24), so nothing notices the child dying mid-batch. If llama-server.exe is OOM-killed mid-batch (plausible on the 4 GB target with a 7B Q4 model — confirmed test hardware), every subsequent server.complete() (vlm_server.rs:145-188) fails at `.context("VLM chat/completions request")?` with a connection error, returning Err for each remaining file. The loop's Err arm (556-559) only does `failed += 1; warn; continue` with no distinction between a dead-server connection error and a per-file decode error and no fallback — so the entire remainder of the library is recorded as failed while a known-good CLI backend sits idle. The up-front self-test vlm_server_payload_ok (pipeline/deep_analyze.rs:350-369) runs ONCE before the loop and only guards the "server rejects the payload format" case; it provides zero protection against mid-batch death, so this is a distinct, un-mitigated failure mode. All preconditions are satisfiable in production. Confidence held at 0.82 (not higher) because triggering requires an actual mid-batch process death rather than being unconditional, which keeps it at the claimed medium severity rather than a guaranteed defect.

Verified against source. In run_deep_analyze_batch (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/deep_analyze.rs:271), both backends are resolved up front: runner=VlmRunner::find().ok() (line 319) and server=VlmServer::start(...) gated by a ONE-TIME payload self-test (lines 339-375). The per-file loop (lines 410-561) at lines 530-532 unconditionally routes through analyze_file_via_server whenever server.as_ref() is Some; the CLI branch (533-534) is reached ONLY when server is None. A grep for try_wait/health/restart/fallback inside this file confirms the loop has NO per-file liveness recheck, NO /health re-probe, NO server restart, and NO CLI salvage — the only fallback logic is the single pre-batch self-test.

VlmServer is a genuine long-lived process: it holds _child: Child with kill_on_drop(true) (vlm_server.rs:24-25,103,136) and loads the model once in start(); it stays resident for the whole batch. If that child dies mid-batch, complete() (vlm_server.rs:145-196) returns Err on .send().await.context(...)? (connection refused, lines 176-183) or on a non-success status (184-187). analyze_file_via_server propagates every complete() via ? (pipeline/deep_analyze.rs:403/412/424). The loop's Err arm (deep_analyze.rs:556-559) merely does failed += 1 plus a tracing::warn! and continues, so EVERY remaining file fails identically. The batch then emits DeepAnalyzeComplete with cancelled:false and a large failed count (563-571), and no IPC Error event is surfaced for the dead server — silent, non-actionable degradation.

The salvage path is real and commonly available: llama-mtmd-cli.exe ships in the same runtime dir as llama-server.exe (vlm.rs:73-74; vlm_server.rs comment 12-13), so runner is typically Some, yet it is never used once server is Some. Both handle_deep_analyze_all (line 221, whole-library WHERE kind IN ('image','video')) and the folder path (line 162/218) reach this live code — not dead code.

Impact lens: this is wasted work / silent failure of the entire remainder of a multi-hour pass, not crash/corruption, consistent with the medium severity. The trigger (server killed by OS low-memory or an image crashing mmproj on the documented 4 GB DirectML box running 7B-Q4 with -ngl 99, vlm_server.rs:90-91) is plausible though not guaranteed — that uncertainty is the only reason confidence is not higher. The logic flaw and its harm-when-triggered are incontrovertible.

confirmed.title: EP-specific quantized model variant is selected from override-blind active_provider() but bound on a different EP — BGE on an OpenVINO/QNN box is the deterministic case
confirmed.description:
resolve_model_path() picks the on-disk model variant from variant_suffix(active_provider()). active_provider() (runtime.rs:315) is RuntimeProbe::detect().provider == pick_provider(...), which IGNORES the gpuExecutionProviderOverride and is purely vendor-derived. But the model wrappers bind their session from the OVERRIDE-AWARE priority_chain (runtime.rs:130, honored identically by configure_session_builder at runtime.rs:381). So the EP used to CHOOSE the file and the EP that actually RUNS it are computed from two different sources of truth and can disagree.

Deterministic trigger (no override needed): BGE. bge_text::default_weights_path() (bge_text.rs:169) calls resolve_model_path("bge_text","bge_small"). On an Intel box with the OpenVINO pack present, active_provider() == OpenVino, so if a hand-dropped bge_small_int8.onnx exists, resolve_model_path returns the OpenVINO-targeted static-INT8 graph — yet BGE hardcodes its session to chain = vec![Cpu] (bge_text.rs:53) and commits that file on the CPU EP. On a Qualcomm box (active_provider()==Qnn) it is worse: resolve_for returns bge_small_qnn.BIN (a QNN context binary, not ONNX), which bge_text.rs commit_from_file() then tries to load on the CPU EP — a guaranteed bind failure (and, per scan.rs:230-235 BGE failing to load is not the aborting class, so search/doc-embedding silently degrades).

Override-driven trigger for SFace/YuNet/RAM++ (which DO bind priority_chain): user on an Intel+OpenVINO box sets gpuExecutionProviderOverride="directml" and hand-drops face_recognition_sface_2021dec_int8.onnx. active_provider() still returns OpenVino (override-blind) → the INT8 variant is selected, but priority_chain([DirectMl,OpenVino,Cpu]) binds DirectML → an OpenVINO-static-INT8 graph is fed to the DirectML EP (degraded/garbage results or bind failure).

The _int8/_qnn variants are hand-dropped (registry never downloads them, registry.rs:520-531), so this requires a power user, which bounds severity — but the BGE OpenVINO/QNN case is a deterministic mismatch on the shipped CPU pin.
confirmed.trigger: Intel box with the OpenVINO Performance Pack installed (active_provider()==OpenVino), with a hand-dropped bge_text/bge_small_int8.onnx (or, on a Qualcomm box, bge_small_qnn.bin): BGE selects the EP-specific variant via active_provider() but binds it on its hardcoded CPU EP, producing wrong embeddings or a commit_from_file failure. Or any SFace/YuNet/RAM++ box where gpuExecutionProviderOverride differs from the vendor-derived active_provider() and a hand-dropped _int8/_qnn variant exists.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make variant selection use the SAME EP the wrapper will bind, not the override-blind active_provider(). Have resolve_model_path consult priority_chain(RuntimeProbe::shared().vendor).into_iter().next() (the override-aware first EP, identical to configure_session_builder's choice) instead of active_provider(). For BGE specifically (which always pins CPU), pass the CPU EP explicitly into resolve_for so it can never select an _int8/_qnn variant for a CPU-EP session.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading the actual source. The claim is genuine: variant selection and session binding are computed from two different, divergeable sources of truth.

(1) VARIANT SELECTION is override-blind. variants.rs:38-40 resolve_model_path() calls resolve_for(dir, stem, active_provider()). active_provider() (runtime.rs:315-318) = RuntimeProbe::detect().provider = pick_provider(vendor, packs...) (runtime.rs:80-85, 419-450). pick_provider NEVER reads read_user_ep_override — it is purely vendor+pack derived. variant_suffix (variants.rs:22-31) maps OpenVino->_int8 (.onnx) and Qnn->_qnn (.bin via variants.rs:46).

(2) SESSION BINDING differs per model. BGE hardcodes chain = vec![Cpu] (bge_text.rs:53) and commit_from_file on CPU (bge_text.rs:73-75), yet its path comes from active_provider() via default_weights_path (bge_text.rs:168-172). SFace (sface.rs:38), YuNet (yunet.rs:48), RAM++ (ram_plus.rs:160) bind from the OVERRIDE-AWARE priority_chain(probe.vendor) (runtime.rs:130-131 reads read_user_ep_override) while still resolving their path from active_provider().

DETERMINISTIC BGE CASE (no override): Intel box + OpenVINO pack present -> pick_provider(Intel,ov=true)=OpenVino (runtime.rs:433-437) -> active_provider()=OpenVino -> resolve_for picks bge_small_int8.onnx if present, but BGE commits it on the CPU EP. Qualcomm + QNN pack -> active_provider()=Qnn -> resolve_for returns bge_small_qnn.bin (a QNN context binary, not ONNX) -> commit_from_file on CPU is a guaranteed bind failure. scan.rs:228-236 aborting set is ONLY face detect/recog (scrfd/arcface) and image embedding (mobileclip) — BGE is NOT included, so the failure silently degrades doc-search/embedding (confirmed).

OVERRIDE-DRIVEN CASE: any SFace/YuNet/RAM++ box where gpuExecutionProviderOverride disagrees with the vendor-derived active_provider() (e.g. Intel box, override=directml) picks the variant for OpenVino but binds DirectML -> EP/file mismatch.

SEVERITY BOUND CONFIRMED: registry.rs:445-453, 78-95, 174-199 download only base .onnx files; registry.rs:520-531 explicitly states _int8/_qnn variants are never downloaded and must be hand-dropped; no code path generates them (grep confirmed). On the default shipped config only the base .onnx exists, so resolve_for falls back to the base graph (variants.rs:52) — no bug. So it requires a power user, correctly bounding severity to medium. But the Intel/OpenVINO BGE mismatch is deterministic with no override needed.

The proposed fix matches the codebase's own established pattern: configure_session_builder (runtime.rs:374-384) and armed_provider (runtime.rs:333-353) BOTH already deliberately use the override-aware priority_chain instead of active_provider() for exactly this reason — the variant resolver is the one place left on the override-blind path. Confidence 0.9 (slightly below certain only because triggering needs a hand-dropped quantized file, and the practical severity of feeding a static-INT8 graph to the CPU EP vs. a hard failure depends on ORT behavior I cannot run here — but the EP/file divergence and the QNN-binary-on-CPU guaranteed failure are incontrovertible from the source).

Independently verified the full mechanic in source. (1) resolve_model_path selects the on-disk variant from active_provider() (variants.rs:39), and active_provider() is RuntimeProbe::detect().provider == pick_provider(...), which is purely vendor+pack-derived and IGNORES gpuExecutionProviderOverride (runtime.rs:315-318, 419-450; read_user_ep_override only feeds priority_chain at 131-147). (2) BGE hardcodes its session to chain = vec![Cpu] (bge_text.rs:53) and commits via commit_from_file (bge_text.rs:74). (3) SFace/YuNet/RAM++ instead bind the override-aware priority_chain(probe.vendor) (sface.rs:38, ram_plus.rs:160) — the same head configure_session_builder uses at runtime.rs:381. So the EP that CHOOSES the file and the EP that RUNS it come from two different sources of truth and can disagree — exactly the divergence the codebase already recognized and fixed for ep_guard (DECISIONS.md:99, "It armed active_provider() ... while the wrappers bind the override-aware priority_chain head"; fix = armed_provider()). The variant resolver was never updated to match that fix, so the same bug class persists here.

BGE deterministic case (no override) is the strongest: on a Qualcomm box with the QNN pack present, active_provider()==Qnn → resolve_for returns bge_small_qnn.bin (variants.rs:46-49). tagging.rs:503 checks wp.exists() (true) then calls BgeText::load, whose commit_from_file tries to load a QNN context binary on a CPU-only chain — a guaranteed parse/bind failure. tagging.rs:508-511 swallows it to None, and scan.rs's abort set covers only face (scrfd/arcface) + image-embedding (mobileclip) at lines 230-235 — BGE is NOT in it — so semantic doc-search silently degrades. The OpenVINO-INT8 sub-case likely runs on CPU (QDQ is CPU-runnable per DECISIONS.md:2781) but on a different graph than tested = silent accuracy drift. Override-driven case for SFace/RAM++ also holds: active_provider() stays OpenVino while priority_chain binds the directml override → OpenVINO-static-INT8 graph fed to the DirectML EP.

Reachability under my lens: the path is genuinely reachable and harmful, NOT dead code/impossible/guarded — but the decisive precondition is a hand-dropped _int8/_qnn file. registry.rs:520-533 and DECISIONS.md:398-418 confirm the engine never downloads or creates these variants; they exist only if a power user manually drops them into the model dir. No default/shipped install produces this state, so the trigger is power-user-gated, which correctly bounds severity to medium (as claimed). Within that precondition the bug is deterministic (BGE/QNN) and the harm (silent search/doc-embedding degradation or bind failure) is real. Confidence held below ~0.9 only because the trigger requires the documented manual variant drop and the most damaging silent-garbage variant (DirectML/INT8) depends on EP-specific runtime behavior I could not exercise on hardware.

The logic divergence the bug describes is REAL and I verified it line-by-line: resolve_model_path (variants.rs:38-39) selects the on-disk variant from override-blind active_provider() (runtime.rs:315-318 -> pick_provider, 419-450), while the wrappers bind from override-aware priority_chain (runtime.rs:130-147). SFace (sface.rs:38), YuNet (yunet.rs:48) and RAM++ all bind priority_chain; BGE pins chain=[Cpu] (bge_text.rs:53) yet still resolves its file via active_provider() (bge_text.rs:169). The _int8/_qnn variants are never downloaded, only hand-dropped (registry.rs:520-532). BGE is NOT in the scan abort set (scan.rs:228-236 checks only face + image-embedding), so a BGE load failure is caught at tagging.rs:508-511 and skipped. All confirmed.

But under the IMPACT lens the claimed harm does not materialize on any reachable path:

1. BGE-on-Qualcomm ("guaranteed bind failure"): the .bin would indeed fail commit_from_file on the CPU EP, but the failure is caught (tagging.rs:508), logged, and the stage skips — semantic doc-search degrades gracefully. No crash/hang/data-loss/corruption. Requires a power user to hand-drop bge_small_qnn.bin onto a Snapdragon box — a self-inflicted misconfiguration of a file the engine never ships.

2. BGE-on-Intel/OpenVINO ("wrong embeddings"): an OpenVINO-targeted static-INT8 ONNX is a standard QDQ/QOperator graph that ORT's CPU EP runs fine; it loads and yields mildly-degraded-but-valid INT8 embeddings in the same 384-d space, not garbage. Minor quality loss at most, and again only after a hand-drop.

3. SFace/YuNet/RAM++ override case: requires THREE simultaneous deliberate actions (Intel+OpenVINO box + override="directml" + hand-dropped _int8.onnx). Even then SFace fails loudly on a dim mismatch (sface.rs:107-112) and YuNet is fail-soft to empty detections (yunet.rs:13-14). Bounded, self-inflicted, recovered.

So this is a genuine two-sources-of-truth design wart with a sound suggested fix, but it is latent/benign: every failure mode is caught, logged, and degrades gracefully, and every trigger demands deliberate hand-dropping of files the engine never installs. It is not a triggerable harmful bug on the shipped configuration, so I refute under the impact lens. Confidence is moderate because the design flaw is real and a reviewer optimizing for correctness (not impact) could reasonably call it 'real'; my refutation rests specifically on the no-harm/benign-degradation criterion this lens requires.

confirmed.title: Concurrent EmbedTextQuery and StartScan race on the single shared .ep_attempt breadcrumb, so a search during scan startup can disarm the EP crash gate before the risky GPU bind crashes
confirmed.description:
ep_guard::arm() and disarm() operate on a single process-global file packs/.ep_attempt (attempt_path(), ep_guard.rs:33). Both the scan path (scan.rs:160/166) and the lazy CLIP-text search path (embed.rs:163/165) arm-then-disarm around their own GPU-EP session binds, and the IPC dispatcher tokio::spawns StartScan (main.rs:557) and EmbedTextQuery (main.rs:625) as independent concurrent tasks with no mutual exclusion. Because the breadcrumb is one shared file (not keyed per call site / per EP), one task's disarm() (std::fs::remove_file) can delete the breadcrumb the OTHER task's arm() just wrote, while that other task is still mid-bind. The crash gate then fails to catch a real native crash.

Concrete sequence on an NVIDIA+CUDA-pack box: (1) StartScan → arm("cuda") writes .ep_attempt, then load_default begins the risky native CUDA session bind on a blocking thread; (2) the user runs a search → EmbedTextQuery spawns; CLIP-text loads (fast/CPU) → arm("cuda") then disarm() removes .ep_attempt; (3) the scan's CUDA bind hard-crashes the process — exactly the crash this gate exists to catch; (4) next launch resolve_poison_at_startup() (ep_guard.rs:80) finds no .ep_attempt → CUDA is NOT disabled → the engine crash-loops on every launch, which is the precise failure mode the gate was built to prevent.
confirmed.trigger: On a box with a guarded GPU EP (CUDA or OpenVINO pack present and not yet crash-disabled), issue a search (EmbedTextQuery) while a scan is starting up its model stack. The search's first-load disarm() deletes the breadcrumb the scan armed; if the scan's GPU bind then crashes, ep_guard never disables the EP and the next launch repeats the crash.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the breadcrumb non-clobbering across concurrent binds. Either serialize all guarded GPU binds behind a single process mutex so only one arm/disarm window is ever open, or key the breadcrumb per concurrent owner (e.g. .ep_attempt.<pid>.<unique-id> containing the EP name) and have resolve_poison_at_startup scan all of them; disarm should remove only the file it created. At minimum, refcount arms so disarm only removes the breadcrumb when the last outstanding arm for that EP completes.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual source. The breadcrumb is a single process-global file: attempt_path() (ep_guard.rs:33-35) joins a fixed ".ep_attempt" with no keying by call site, PID, or EP name. disarm() (ep_guard.rs:71-75) unconditionally remove_file()s that one file with no owner check, no refcount, and no per-EP discrimination. arm() (ep_guard.rs:54-67) just writes the EP name to that same file.

Two independent arm/disarm windows wrap separate guarded-EP binds: scan.rs:160/166 wraps ModelStack::load_default (which binds SFace/YuNet/MobileCLIP-image/RAM++/BGE/scene-labeler ORT sessions — confirmed in tagging.rs:426-555), and embed.rs:163/165 wraps the lazily-loaded clip_text::ClipText::load (embed.rs:133-167, behind a OnceLock<Mutex<Option<ClipText>>>). Crucially, load_default does NOT load clip_text — it loads the MobileCLIP image encoder — so the search path genuinely performs its OWN separate GPU-EP session bind even while a scan is mid-load.

Both armed_provider() calls (runtime.rs:333-353) resolve to the SAME guarded EP (e.g. "cuda") from the shared RuntimeProbe + live pack/disable state, so both arm() write the same string to the same file and both disarm() delete that same file.

The two paths run as genuinely concurrent, mutually-unsynchronized tasks: the stdio dispatch loop (main.rs:332-360) awaits handle_line, which for StartScan (main.rs:557) and EmbedTextQuery (main.rs:625) only tokio::spawns the handler and returns immediately, then reads the next frame. The scan's load_default runs on a spawn_blocking thread inside a 120s timeout (scan.rs:161-165), so it is long-lived and overlaps a subsequently dispatched EmbedTextQuery. embed.rs has no scan_state/busy gate and no shared model-load mutex (its only lock is the CLIP-text-local TEXT_MODEL mutex), so nothing serializes the two binds.

Race interleaving (re-derived independently): (1) scan arm("cuda") writes .ep_attempt, begins risky native CUDA bind; (2) search task arms (rewrites same file, harmless), ClipText loads fast, then disarm() removes .ep_attempt; (3) scan's CUDA bind hard-crashes the process — the exact crash the gate exists to catch; (4) next launch resolve_poison_at_startup() (ep_guard.rs:80-101) finds no .ep_attempt -> CUDA not disabled -> crash-loop on every launch. This is precisely the failure mode the gate was built to prevent, and the in-code comments at scan.rs:151-158 and embed.rs:154-161 explicitly acknowledge these are two SEPARATE arm windows around the shared breadcrumb. The suggested fix (per-owner keying / refcount / serialize binds behind one mutex) is exactly what is missing.

Severity assessment: the logic defect is incontrovertible. The trigger window is real but narrow (user must search during the brief model-load startup window; the CLIP-text bind is fast and often CPU/cached so its disarm fires quickly; a guarded GPU pack must be present, un-disabled, and the scan's bind must actually crash within that window). That narrowness justifies medium, not high — but it does not refute the bug. Confidence held at 0.85 rather than higher only because the empirical hit rate of the precise interleaving is hardware/timing dependent; the code-level defect itself is certain.

The mechanical race is real but the harmful outcome is not triggerable in production.

VERIFIED TRUE (the mechanics):
- ep_guard.rs:33-34 — `.ep_attempt` is a single shared file, not keyed per call-site nor refcounted.
- ep_guard.rs:71-74 — `disarm()` unconditionally `std::fs::remove_file`s it.
- scan.rs:159-166 arms `armed_provider()` then disarms around `ModelStack::load_default`; embed.rs:162-165 arms the same `armed_provider()` then disarms around `ClipText::load`. Both pick the same EP (cuda on a CUDA-pack box).
- main.rs:557 and main.rs:625 `tokio::spawn` StartScan and EmbedTextQuery as independent tasks; each load runs on its own `spawn_blocking` thread (scan.rs:163, embed.rs:133). I found NO process-wide mutex serializing ORT session creation — each wrapper independently calls `with_execution_providers`. So embed's `disarm()` CAN delete the breadcrumb scan armed. ENABLE_CLIP=true (scene_vocab.rs:140) so the embed arm/disarm is reachable, and no client-side gate blocks search during a scan.

WHY IT IS NOT A GENUINE/HARMFUL BUG (trigger/reachability lens):
The harm requires embed's CLIP-text CUDA bind to SUCCEED (reach disarm at embed.rs:165) WHILE scan's CUDA bind HARD-CRASHES. But clip_text.rs:33-44 builds the session via the SAME `priority_chain` + `execution_providers_for_chain` as every scan model, which pushes the identical `CUDAExecutionProvider` (runtime.rs:265) loading the identical pack DLLs from the same `Models/packs/cuda`. The bug's premise that "CLIP-text loads (fast/CPU)" is factually wrong — on a CUDA box CLIP-text attempts the SAME CUDA bind.

The gate's stated purpose (ep_guard.rs:1-9; DECISIONS.md:112) is a categorical native crash at bind time — a bad `onnxruntime_providers_cuda.dll`, driver mismatch, or device-init OOM. These crash at provider-library load / device-context init on the FIRST risky CUDA bind in the process, and are not model-data-dependent. A hard crash means the crashing task never reaches its `disarm()`, so ITS breadcrumb survives → gate works next launch. For the clobber to be harmful, the sibling task must have FULLY SUCCEEDED its own identical-DLL/identical-device CUDA bind — which is direct evidence the DLLs/driver/device are healthy, contradicting the premise that the other identical bind then hard-crashes the process. There is no realistic interleaving where one CUDA session loads the same provider DLLs cleanly and a sibling crashes the process milliseconds later.

So this is a theoretically-real shared-file race whose harmful precondition is self-contradictory for the failure class the gate targets; in production the first guarded bind to crash leaves its own breadcrumb and the gate still bounds the blast radius to one crash. The suggested fix (refcount / per-owner keying) is a reasonable robustness hardening, but the claimed crash-loop is not reachable. Confidence held below 0.9 because a non-deterministic transient bind-time OOM on a memory-starved 4 GB box is the one residual interleaving I cannot fully exclude — but that is outside the bad-DLL/driver class the gate and this bug describe, and even there the clobber still requires the sibling's identical-device bind to have succeeded first.

Verified all load-bearing facts in source. (1) ep_guard.rs:33-35 — the breadcrumb is a single process-global file `packs/.ep_attempt` (one path, not keyed per call site / per EP). (2) ep_guard.rs:54-67 `arm()` does `std::fs::write(p, ep)` and ep_guard.rs:71-75 `disarm()` does an UNCONDITIONAL `std::fs::remove_file(p)` — no refcount, no per-owner keying, no check that this task wrote it. (3) Both the scan path (scan.rs:159-166: `arm(armed_provider)` → `spawn_blocking(ModelStack::load_default)` → `disarm`) and the lazy CLIP-text search path (embed.rs:162-165: `arm(armed_provider)` → `ClipText::load` → `disarm`) arm/disarm this same shared file around their own native GPU-EP session binds. load_default (tagging.rs:426+) does multiple risky native ORT binds (SFace/YuNet/MobileCLIP). (4) main.rs:557 and main.rs:625 `tokio::spawn` StartScan and EmbedTextQuery as independent concurrent tasks with NO mutual exclusion. (5) `ENABLE_CLIP = true` (scene_vocab.rs:140), so the embed.rs arm/disarm path is LIVE, not dead code. (6) Grep confirmed there is NO global mutex serializing ORT/EP binds in models/ — nothing prevents the two bind windows from overlapping. The interleaving is therefore genuinely reachable: scan arms+begins its slow native CUDA bind on a blocking thread; a search issued during the up-to-120s model-load window (search box is not disabled, and first-launch CLIP-matrix encode is noted to take 20+s) spawns, binds CLIP-text, and `disarm()` removes the live breadcrumb; if the scan's bind then hard-crashes, resolve_poison_at_startup (ep_guard.rs:80) finds no `.ep_attempt`, CUDA is NOT disabled, and the engine crash-loops on every launch — the exact failure the gate exists to prevent. IMPACT lens: the harm is real, not masked/benign — `disarm`'s unconditional remove deletes a breadcrumb another in-flight bind depends on, and the gate silently fails open precisely when needed. One narrative inaccuracy in the claim ('CLIP-text loads fast/CPU') is immaterial: the embed path actually binds the SAME guarded GPU EP via armed_provider(), but the harm comes from its disarm() deleting the live breadcrumb regardless. Confidence held below 0.9 because triggering requires a low-probability conjunction (a rare native guarded-GPU-bind hard-crash AND a search issued during the bind window AND the embed task's disarm landing while the scan bind is still in flight); the design defect and its harmful consequence are nonetheless genuine and unguarded, consistent with the medium severity. The suggested fixes (serialize guarded binds behind one mutex, or per-owner-keyed breadcrumbs + refcounted disarm) are appropriate.

confirmed.title: WordPiece basic_tokenize lowercases ASCII only — non-ASCII document text degrades to [UNK], corrupting BGE text embeddings
confirmed.description: basic_tokenize is constructed with lower_case=true (BgeText::load passes true) yet lowercases each char with `ch.to_ascii_lowercase()`, which only folds ASCII A–Z and leaves every non-ASCII letter unchanged. HuggingFace BertTokenizer(do_lower_case=True) — which the vocab.txt for BGE-small-en-v1.5 is built for — lowercases full Unicode. Because the vocab pieces are stored lowercased, an unlowered uppercase non-ASCII character (e.g. 'É', 'Ü', 'Ö', 'Ñ', or any uppercase Cyrillic/Greek) will not match any wordpiece prefix, so wordpiece() falls through to `[UNK]` for the whole token (see line 145). This is on the LIVE scan path: pipeline/tagging.rs line 1253 calls BgeText::embed(&text) on extracted document text and persists the result into text_embeddings, which powers Phase-4b semantic document search. The module comment scopes itself to 'the common English case', but this also breaks ordinary Western-European accented text (names, café, naïve, München, José, résumé), not just non-Latin scripts, so common real-world documents get materially wrong embeddings.
confirmed.trigger: Scan a document (PDF/Office/text) whose extracted text contains an uppercase accented or non-ASCII letter, e.g. a heading 'CAFÉ MENU' or a name 'JOSÉ' or 'MÜLLER'. The 'É'/'Ü' is not lowercased, fails to match the lowercased vocab, and the containing token becomes [UNK]; the resulting BGE text embedding diverges from the correct one, degrading semantic search hits for that document.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Replace `ch.to_ascii_lowercase()` with full Unicode lowering. Since `ch` is a char and lowering can expand to multiple chars, iterate the lowered chars: e.g. build the per-char output via `ch.to_lowercase()` (push each produced char), or pre-lowercase the whole string with `text.to_lowercase()` before the char loop when lower_case is set. This matches BertTokenizer(do_lower_case=True) for non-ASCII input.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against source. (1) Citation is exact: wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:96 `let c = if self.lower_case { ch.to_ascii_lowercase() } else { ch };` in basic_tokenize (line 92), which only folds ASCII A-Z; 'É','Ü','Ö','Ñ' and all non-ASCII letters pass through unchanged. (2) lower_case=true is in fact passed: bge_text.rs:44 calls WordPieceTokenizer::from_vocab_file(vocab, true), so this branch is always active for BGE. (3) Live scan path confirmed end-to-end: tagging.rs:1253 `bge.embed(&text)` runs on extracted document text (FileKind::Doc/Pdf, up to 256 KB per doc_extract.rs:14) -> stored to tagged.text_embedding (1254) -> persisted by dbwriter.rs:199-202 and 328-336 into the text_embeddings table (model "bge_small_en_v1_5"), which powers Phase-4b semantic doc search. (4) Whole-token poisoning confirmed: wordpiece() line 145 `None => return vec![self.unk_id]` returns [UNK] for the ENTIRE whitespace token on the first unmatchable prefix, so even already-matched leading pieces (e.g. "caf" of "cafÉ") are discarded. (5) No Unicode normalization exists anywhere in the tokenizer (grep found no nfd/nfc/strip_accents/to_lowercase; Cargo has no unicode-normalization dep). The BGE-small-en-v1.5 vocab (bert-uncased family, downloaded from Xenova at runtime, not in repo) is built lowercased, so an unlowered 'É' cannot match a lowercased vocab piece -> [UNK]. Mean-pooling in embed() (bge_text.rs:139-148) means replacing content tokens with [UNK] materially shifts the resulting vector; for French/German/Spanish/Portuguese documents, accented characters are pervasive, so a large fraction of tokens degrade. The bug is genuine, reachable on the default scan path, and harmful to semantic-search quality. Two refinements that do not change the verdict: (a) the impact is actually slightly UNDERSTATED — HF's uncased BasicTokenizer also strips accents at vocab-build time, so even lowercase accented text (e.g. "café") fails here because the vocab has no accented pieces; (b) consequently the finder's suggested fix `ch.to_lowercase()` / `text.to_lowercase()` is INCOMPLETE — it folds É->é but é still won't match an accent-stripped vocab; a faithful fix needs NFD decomposition + combining-mark removal to mirror BertTokenizer(do_lower_case=True). The incomplete remedy does not invalidate the defect. Confidence is 0.86 rather than higher only because I could not directly inspect the runtime-downloaded vocab.txt to byte-confirm the absence of accented pieces, but the bert-uncased lineage makes that near-certain, and the uppercase-mismatch mechanism the finder cites holds regardless of the accent-strip subtlety.

Verified against source.

(1) Trigger/lower_case precondition: bge_text.rs:44 constructs the tokenizer with WordPieceTokenizer::from_vocab_file(vocab_txt, true) — lower_case=true, exactly as claimed.

(2) The buggy lowering: wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:96 — `let c = if self.lower_case { ch.to_ascii_lowercase() } else { ch };`. char::to_ascii_lowercase only folds ASCII A–Z and is a no-op for any codepoint >= 0x80, so 'É' (U+00C9), 'Ü', 'Ñ', uppercase Cyrillic/Greek, etc. pass through unchanged. HF BertTokenizer(do_lower_case=True) — which an uncased English vocab like BGE-small-en-v1.5 is built for — lowercases the full Unicode range (and, for the standard uncased vocab, also strips accents). This implementation does neither, so it does NOT honor the do_lower_case=True contract it advertises. The module comment (line 7) even concedes it only targets "the common English case."

(3) Fall-through to [UNK]: wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:119-149 greedily matches lowercased vocab pieces; on ANY unmatchable prefix it returns vec![self.unk_id] for the whole token (line 145). Since the lowercased vocab cannot contain an uppercase non-ASCII piece, a token like "josÉ" or "CAFÉ" cannot be matched and becomes [UNK]. Confirmed.

(4) Reachability on the live scan path: tagging.rs:1241-1267 — for any non-cloud FileKind::Doc|Pdf with non-empty extracted text, bge.embed(&text) is called at line 1253 on the worker thread, and the result is persisted into tagged.text_embedding, which powers Phase-4b semantic doc search (bge_text.rs:3-6). `text` is raw extracted document content with no upstream Unicode normalization/accent-strip (grep for normalize/NFD/strip_accents in the tokenizer found nothing). Preconditions are trivially satisfiable: real documents routinely contain uppercase accented chars (all-caps headings "CAFÉ", names "JOSÉ"/"MÜLLER", German/French/Spanish/Portuguese text). The path is reachable and the inputs are ordinary, not an impossible/guarded state.

(5) Harm: each affected token degrades to [UNK], perturbing the mean-pooled 384-d embedding. The "whole document corrupted" framing overstates the typical case — a single accented heading among hundreds of ASCII words is robust under mean-pooling — but a predominantly accented/non-English document (or a names-heavy list) has many tokens collapse to [UNK], materially degrading the embedding and its retrieval quality versus the correct HF tokenization. The suggested fix (use ch.to_lowercase(), which can expand to multiple chars, or pre-lowercase the whole string) is correct.

Verdict: real. The central technical claim is incontrovertible (to_ascii_lowercase does not satisfy do_lower_case=True for non-ASCII and forces [UNK]), the cited runtime path is genuinely reachable with realistic inputs, and the effect is harmful for accented/non-English document embeddings. Confidence held at 0.85 (not higher) only because the practical severity is more "degraded for non-English-heavy docs" than the report's "corrupting … materially wrong" blanket framing, and I could not inspect the actual shipped vocab.txt (not checked into the repo) to quantify the per-piece impact — but neither caveat undermines the core bug.

VERIFIED REAL on the IMPACT lens, with one caveat about the suggested fix's completeness.

Code facts verified:
1. wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:96 — basic_tokenize lowercases with `ch.to_ascii_lowercase()`, which (per Rust's char::to_ascii_lowercase) folds ONLY ASCII A-Z and leaves every non-ASCII char (É, Ü, Ö, Ñ, Cyrillic, Greek) unchanged. Confirmed against the contrast at clip_tokenizer.rs:108 which correctly uses full-Unicode `to_lowercase()` — the codebase applied the two inconsistently.
2. bge_text.rs:44 — BgeText::load calls `WordPieceTokenizer::from_vocab_file(vocab, true)`, so lower_case IS true at runtime, as claimed.
3. Live scan path confirmed: tagging.rs:1253 calls `bge.embed(&text)` on extracted document text inside the per-file tagging stage and persists the result into `tagged.text_embedding` (line 1254), which the dbwriter writes to the `text_embeddings` table powering Phase-4b semantic doc search (bge_text.rs header comment).
4. wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:140-145 — the WordPiece loop is all-or-nothing per whitespace token: if any sub-slice down to a single char fails the vocab lookup, the WHOLE word returns `[UNK]`. So a word containing an unlowered É/Ü loses all its semantic content, exactly as claimed at line 145.

Impact is genuine: BGE-small-en-v1.5 ships the bert-base-uncased vocabulary, whose pieces are stored lowercased. An unlowered uppercase non-ASCII char cannot match any lowercased piece → token degrades to [UNK] → the persisted text embedding diverges from the canonical one, weakening semantic-search recall for documents with such text (e.g. a 'CAFÉ MENU' heading, 'JOSÉ', 'MÜLLER'). This is data quality degradation on the live scan path, not a crash/leak, and the embedding is still finite/normalized/deterministic — so it's a correctness/recall regression, matching the stated 'medium' severity, not catastrophic corruption.

Important caveat that does NOT refute the bug but qualifies the fix: I reproduced HF's bert-base-uncased BasicTokenizer behavior. With do_lower_case=True, BERT also runs _run_strip_accents (NFD + drop Mn), so the vocab form of CAFÉ is 'cafe', JOSÉ→'jose', MÜLLER→'muller', and even already-lowercase café→'cafe', München→'munchen'. The engine never strips accents, so even the lowercase accented words (café, naïve, résumé, München) ALSO fall through to [UNK] regardless of case. This means: (a) the harm is actually BROADER than the claim states, and (b) the suggested fix (replace to_ascii_lowercase with full-Unicode to_lowercase) is INSUFFICIENT — it yields café/müller, which still miss the accent-stripped vocab pieces cafe/muller. A correct fix needs Unicode lowercasing AND NFD accent stripping to mirror BertTokenizer(do_lower_case=True). The bug claim itself (degraded embeddings for non-ASCII/accented text on the live path) is nonetheless confirmed real and triggerable. Confidence held below 0.9 only because the exact magnitude depends on the shipped vocab.txt (not checked into the repo; downloaded at runtime) and because per-document harm is partial (non-accented tokens in the same doc still embed correctly).

confirmed.title: BGE mean-pool indexes attention_mask by the model's output sequence length, panicking when a re-export's seq dim exceeds the input token count
confirmed.description: After inference, `seq` is taken from the model output tensor (`shape[1]`, line 136) and the pooling loop iterates `for t in 0..seq`, reading `enc.attention_mask[t]` at line 140 and `enc.type/ids` indirectly. But `enc.attention_mask` has length exactly `n = enc.ids.len()` (the number of tokens actually fed in: `[CLS] … [SEP]`, confirmed in wordpiece_tokenizer.rs encode() where `attention_mask = vec![1i64; ids.len()]`). The post-run contract check at line 130 only validates `shape.len() == 3` and `shape[2] == HIDDEN` (the hidden dim) — it never checks that the output sequence dim `shape[1]` equals the input token count `n`. The shipped `bge_small.onnx` emits a dynamic seq dim so `seq == n` and the code is safe, but this module is explicitly designed to bind alternate re-exports (file header: 'a re-export with different names still binds'; default_weights_path resolves `_int8`/`_qnn` variant siblings). A BGE re-export with a STATIC padded sequence length (e.g. always 256 — common for fixed-shape ONNX exports targeting some EPs/quantizers) yields `seq = 256` while a short document tokenizes to `n` < 256, so `enc.attention_mask[t]` for `t >= n` is out of bounds and the engine PANICS on the worker thread. Note the `data[t * HIDDEN + h]` read at line 146 is safe (bounded by the tensor's own length), so the crash is specifically the `enc.attention_mask[t]` access. This is a hard panic where every other model wrapper in this subsystem (SFace line 107, YuNet load-time name check) fail SOFT or bail with a clean error.
confirmed.trigger: Install/point BGE at a re-exported `bge_small` ONNX whose `last_hidden_state` output has a fixed/padded sequence dimension larger than the number of tokens for a given input (e.g. a static-shape seq=256 export), then run Deep Analyze / document tagging on any short text string (so n < 256). tagging.rs:1253 calls `bge.embed(&text)`; the loop reads `enc.attention_mask[t]` for t up to seq-1=255 while attention_mask.len() == n, panicking the scan worker.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Clamp the pooling loop to the input token count and/or assert the seq dims agree. Either bail loudly when `shape[1] as usize != n` (add to the existing contract check at line 130), or bound the loop with `for t in 0..seq.min(enc.attention_mask.len())`. Bailing matches the file's existing 'fail loud on wrong-variant export' posture used for the hidden-dim check.

verdicts.reasoning:
I read bge_text.rs (the cited file) and wordpiece_tokenizer.rs and variants.rs, and re-derived the logic from scratch.

CONFIRMED sub-facts: (1) wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:84 sets `attention_mask = vec![1i64; ids.len()]`, so `enc.attention_mask.len() == enc.ids.len() == n`. (2) bge_text.rs:89 sets `n = enc.ids.len()`. (3) The pooling loop at lines 139-148 reads `enc.attention_mask[t]` for `t in 0..seq` where `seq = shape[1]` (line 136). (4) The contract check at line 130 indeed only validates `shape.len()==3` and `shape[2]==HIDDEN`, never that `shape[1]==n`.

THE FATAL FLAW IN THE CLAIM: The bug requires the model's OUTPUT sequence dim `seq` to exceed the INPUT token count `n` while reaching the pooling loop. That is unreachable here because:

- The engine does NOT pad the input to MAX_SEQ. Lines 90-97 build `ids`/`mask`/`type_ids` as `Array2::<i64>::zeros((1, n))` — the input tensors are sized to the ACTUAL token count `n`, not 256. MAX_SEQ (line 27, used at line 88) is only the truncation CAP passed to `encode(text, MAX_SEQ)`; it bounds `n <= 256`, it does not pad up to 256. So ONNX Runtime is fed a `[1, n]` input.

- A BERT-family encoder (BGE) emits `last_hidden_state` with sequence dim equal to its INPUT sequence dim (one hidden vector per input position). There is no architectural mechanism by which feeding `n` positions yields a `>n`-length output. For a dynamic-shape export, `seq == n` is an invariant, and the access is safe.

- The claim's specific trigger — a STATIC fixed-shape export with seq dim hard-pinned to 256 — is self-defeating. A graph with a static input shape `[1, 256]` REJECTS the engine's `[1, n]` input (n != 256) at `session.run()` (line 120) with a dimension-mismatch error, which propagates cleanly via `.map_err(classify_inference_error)?` at line 122. The pooling loop is never reached, so there is no panic — the engine bails with a clean Err, matching the soft-fail posture the claim says is absent.

So both export classes the finder posits are safe: dynamic-shape gives `seq == n` (safe loop); static-shape rejects the mismatched variable-length input at run() (clean Err, no panic). The finder's premise implicitly assumes the harness pads input to a fixed 256 (common in other inference code), but this engine deliberately does not — which is precisely what guarantees `seq == n`.

I docked confidence from certainty only because I am relying on the standard ORT/BERT contract (static input shape rejects mismatched input; encoder output seq == input seq) rather than executing against an actual pathological re-export. The missing `shape[1]==n` assertion at line 130 is a minor defensive-robustness gap worth the one-line fix, but it does NOT cause the claimed index/bounds panic because the triggering condition (seq > n at the loop) is not reachable. Refuted as a real, triggerable, harmful bug.

Verified every cited line in platforms/windows/src/engine/src/models/bge_text.rs. The defect is real: line 136 takes seq=shape[1] from the OUTPUT tensor; the pooling loop (lines 139-140) does `for t in 0..seq { let m = enc.attention_mask[t] ...}`, indexing attention_mask by the output seq length. The post-run contract check at line 130 (`shape.len() != 3 || shape[2] != HIDDEN`) validates only rank and hidden dim — it never checks shape[1] == n. attention_mask length is provably exactly n: wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:84 `let attention_mask = vec![1i64; ids.len()];`. So if the output seq dim exceeds n, line 140 is a hard out-of-bounds index panic. Confirmed line 146 `data[t*HIDDEN+h]` is bounded by the tensor's own data length, so the crash is specifically the attention_mask[t] access, exactly as claimed.

Reachability (my lens): inputs are fed at length n, NOT padded (lines 90-92, `Array2::zeros((1, n))`), so a normal dynamic-axis transformer export emits seq==n and is safe — matching the claim that the shipped bge_small.onnx is fine. The bug requires a re-export whose output last_hidden_state has a statically padded seq dim larger than n WHILE the input axis stays dynamic (e.g. a graph that buckets/pads internally). A fully static-shape export (input also pinned to 256) would instead reject the (1,n) input at session.run with a shape-mismatch Err, which is handled SOFT at tagging.rs:1255 — not a panic. So the panic-triggering geometry is narrower than the description's "static seq=256" framing implies. However, this is a SUPPORTED configuration: the module header explicitly states it binds re-exports with different names, and variants.rs (resolve_model_path) is purpose-built to load alternate _int8/_qnn exports, which commonly require static shapes. The call path is live (tagging.rs:1253 bge.embed(&text) during doc tagging), and an index panic there is uncatchable by the surrounding Err handler, crashing the scan worker — unlike SFace (sface.rs:107, ENG-69) and YuNet (yunet.rs:13) which fail soft/loud. Previously logged as ENG-66 (manual triage, never refuted). The defect and missing guard are genuine and harmful; I dock confidence because the panic requires a specific non-default export geometry (dynamic input axis + padded output seq) and a user-installed non-shipped model, so it is not reachable with default shipped weights.

Verified the cited logic directly. In bge_text.rs the post-run contract check (line 130) validates only `shape.len()==3` and `shape[2]==HIDDEN`; it never checks `shape[1]==n`. The pooling loop (line 139) iterates `for t in 0..seq` where `seq = shape[1] as usize` (the OUTPUT seq dim, line 136) and reads `enc.attention_mask[t]` at line 140. I confirmed in wordpiece_tokenizer.rs:84 that `attention_mask = vec![1i64; ids.len()]`, and in bge_text.rs:90-97 the input tensors are sized `(1, n)` with NO padding, so `attention_mask.len() == n`. Therefore if the output seq dim exceeds `n`, line 140 is an out-of-bounds index → Rust panic. The claim's assertion that `data[t*HIDDEN+h]` (line 146) is safe is also correct: `data` length is `seq*HIDDEN` from try_extract_tensor (line 128), so only `enc.attention_mask[t]` is the OOB site. Panic propagation confirmed: tagging.rs:1253 uses `match bge.embed(&text)` which handles `Err`, but a panic is not an `Err`; there is no catch_unwind around embed (the only catch_unwind, tagging.rs:1106, wraps image decode). The panic unwinds the `tokio::spawn` worker task (tagging.rs:772). Cargo.toml:239 sets `panic = "unwind"`, so the process does not abort, but the worker's `while let` loop terminates permanently — that worker stops draining the queue, and with a static-shape export every short doc reproduces it, killing all doc workers. The SFace contrast holds: sface.rs:102/107 does `data.to_vec()` + length check + clean bail, never indexing by an external length — BGE is the outlier that fails hard. The defensive gap and the suggested fix (assert shape[1]==n at the contract check, or bound the loop with seq.min(attention_mask.len())) are both correct.

REACHABILITY/HARM caveats that temper confidence: (1) The shipped model (Xenova/bge-small-en-v1.5, MODELS.md:71-75) is a dynamic-seq export fetched via SHA256-verified download (downloader.rs:346), so the default stack always has seq==n and is safe. (2) The bug requires a manually-supplied/alternate export (variants.rs `_int8`/`_qnn` siblings or a replaced bge_small.onnx — a documented-supported path, DECISIONS.md:399) whose INPUT seq is dynamic/accepts the `(1,n)` tensor but whose OUTPUT seq is statically padded larger than n. This is an unusual export shape: the common static-quantized variant fixes BOTH input and output to a fixed length, in which case the `(1,n)` input (n != fixed) would instead be rejected by `session.run` (line 122) as a clean Err — not the OOB panic. So the precise trigger is narrow and owner-gated (no shipped/verified model hits it), but it is a genuine, triggerable index/bounds panic on the scan worker for a model class the module explicitly intends to bind, with no protective check. Real but low-reachability.

confirmed.title: applyRestructure cannot carry a large plan: 1 MB outbound command cap rejects the same moves the engine just sent in a multi-MB restructurePlan (asymmetric framing)
confirmed.symbol: EngineClient.SendCommandAsync / MaxIpcFrameBytes (and engine MAX_FRAME_BYTES in main.rs:330)
confirmed.description: The IPC framing caps are asymmetric in a way that breaks the documented product target. Engine->app events are unbounded on the wire and the app reads frames up to MaxFrameChars = 32 MiB (EngineClient.cs:64). The engine emits the full restructurePlan in one reliable frame (commands/restructure.rs:263, sink.send().await). A plan for a large library can be tens of MB of moves (each RestructureMove serializes to ~250-400 bytes with full Windows source+destination paths + reason), and it is received and rendered fine. But the RETURN trip, applyRestructure, carries that SAME moves array (RestructureView.ApplyAsync sends ALL selected moves in one frame with no chunking, ApplyRestructureAsync at EngineClient.Commands.cs:632 -> SendCommandAsync). SendCommandAsync hard-rejects any frame whose bytes exceed MaxIpcFrameBytes = 1_000_000 and returns Task.FromException (EngineClient.Commands.cs:31-36); the engine's own inbound bounded_read_line cap (MAX_FRAME_BYTES = 1 MiB, main.rs:330) would also reject it. So the effective apply ceiling is ~3,000-4,000 moves (~1 MB), far below the 'tens of thousands of files'/'~200k moves' target the receive-side comment (EngineClient.cs:55-64) explicitly designed the 32 MB read cap to support. When the user selects more than ~1 MB worth of moves and clicks Apply, the moves are NOT applied and the user sees the misleading message 'Couldn't apply - the engine isn't responding ... Your files were not touched' (RestructureView.xaml.cs:639-642) even though the engine is healthy and the plan was valid.
confirmed.trigger: Plan a restructure on a library large enough that the selected moves serialize to >1,000,000 bytes (roughly >3,500-4,000 files with typical path lengths, or fewer with long paths/reasons), then click Apply / Apply (symlinks). SendCommandAsync throws InvalidOperationException('IPC frame too large'); ApplyAsync's catch surfaces 'the engine isn't responding' and nothing is moved. The plan is displayable (32 MB receive cap) but unappliable (1 MB send cap).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Chunk applyRestructure on the app side: split sel into batches that each stay under MaxIpcFrameBytes (e.g. by estimated serialized size or a fixed move count like 2,000) and send sequential applyRestructure frames, accumulating RestructureApplyResult.applied/failed across batches before reporting to the user. The engine handler already appends per-batch results, so chunking is transparent to it. Alternatively raise both caps (app MaxIpcFrameBytes and engine MAX_FRAME_BYTES) symmetrically and confirm the Windows pipe-buffer deadlock concern (the 1 MB cap's stated rationale) is mitigated by the app's independent stdout drain. Chunking is preferred so a single oversized frame can never approach the pipe-buffer deadlock the cap guards against.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified the full round-trip from source. (1) App outbound cap: EngineClient.Commands.cs:21 MaxIpcFrameBytes = 1_000_000; lines 31-36 hard-reject any oversized frame via Task.FromException(InvalidOperationException) BEFORE the precondition/write. (2) App inbound cap: EngineClient.cs:64 MaxFrameChars = 32 MiB, with a comment (lines 55-63) explicitly stating it was raised from 1 MiB to hold a full plan for the ~200k-move product target and noting outbound plan events are unbounded on the wire — confirming the asymmetry is intentional on receive but unmitigated on send. (3) Engine inbound cap: main.rs:330 MAX_FRAME_BYTES = 1024*1024 (1 MiB), enforced by bounded_read_line; oversize commands are rejected+drained (main.rs:367-378), so even raising the app cap alone would not help. (4) Plan emission: restructure.rs:228-264 sends the entire RestructurePlan (all moves) in one outbound frame — received fine under 32 MiB. (5) Unchunked apply: ApplyRestructureAsync (Commands.cs:632-633) wraps ALL moves into one ApplyRestructureCommand; RestructureView.xaml.cs ApplyAsync (lines 620-631) builds `sel` from every selected move with no chunking and sends in one call; the whole command line is serialized by IpcCoder.EncodeLine and its byte length checked against the 1 MB cap. (6) Misleading UX confirmed verbatim: RestructureView.xaml.cs:639-642 catch surfaces "Couldn't apply - the engine isn't responding ... Your files were not touched" when SendCommandAsync throws the oversize InvalidOperationException — the engine is in fact healthy and the plan valid. (7) Per-move size: RestructureMove (CommandPayload.cs:67-80) carries fileID + full Windows source/destination paths + category + tier + confidence + prose reason, ~250-400 B serialized, so ~3-4k moves saturate 1 MB — far below the documented "tens of thousands"/"~200k" target. (8) Fix feasibility: engine apply() (restructure_apply.rs:65-93) iterates moves accumulating applied/failed counters, so sequential batched applies are transparent to it. The bug is genuine, reachable at the explicit product scale, and harmful (headline feature silently unusable on large libraries with a misdirecting error). Severity medium is apt: no data corruption (apply never starts), but the feature fails at design scale. Confidence held just below certainty only because triggering requires a library large enough to exceed 1 MB of selected moves (thousands of files) — plausible and the stated target, but not something I executed at runtime.

Verified all cited locations independently; the asymmetric-framing bug is genuine, reachable, and harmful.

EVIDENCE:
1. App OUTBOUND cap = 1 MB: EngineClient.Commands.cs:21 `MaxIpcFrameBytes = 1_000_000`. SendCommandAsync hard-rejects oversized frames at lines 31-36 via `Task.FromException(new InvalidOperationException(...))`.
2. Engine INBOUND cap = 1 MiB: main.rs:330 `const MAX_FRAME_BYTES: usize = 1024 * 1024`; oversized inbound frames rejected as BoundedRead::Oversized (lines 367-378). So raising only the app cap is insufficient — both must move, exactly as the fix notes.
3. App RECEIVE cap = 32 MiB: EngineClient.cs:64 `MaxFrameChars = 32 * 1024 * 1024`, and its comment (lines 55-63) explicitly says it was raised to hold a full plan for the product target "~200k moves" / "tens of thousands of files" while acknowledging "The engine's MAX_FRAME_BYTES guard only bounds INBOUND commands." This is the asymmetry, documented in the source itself.
4. Engine emits the WHOLE plan in one outbound frame: restructure.rs:263 `sink.send(IpcEvent::now(EventPayload::RestructurePlan(...)))` — unbounded on the wire.
5. Apply sends ALL selected moves in one frame, NO chunking: ApplyRestructureAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:632-633) is a one-line passthrough to SendCommandAsync; ApplyAsync (RestructureView.xaml.cs:620-631) builds `sel` from every selected move and passes it whole. Grep confirms zero chunking/batching logic in the apply path.
6. Misleading message confirmed: RestructureView.xaml.cs:639-642 catch surfaces "Couldn't apply - the engine isn't responding ... Your files were not touched" — but the throw is a synchronous client-side size-guard InvalidOperationException, not an engine failure. The engine is healthy and the plan was valid.
7. Per-move size: RestructureMove (CommandPayload.cs:67-80) carries fileID + Source (full Windows path) + Destination (full Windows path) + Category + Tier + Confidence + Reason. ~250-400 B/move is accurate, yielding a ~3,000-4,000-move apply ceiling at 1 MB.

The product is explicitly built for "tens of thousands of files" (root CLAUDE.md) and the receive-cap comment cites "~200k moves" as the supported target — so plans far exceeding 4,000 moves are squarely within the intended workload, not an impossible/dead-code edge. The plan is already received and rendered (32 MB receive cap), so the user can readily select >1 MB of moves; no upstream guard chunks or caps the apply set. The trigger is satisfiable in production and the harm (no files moved + a dialog that falsely blames the engine and claims files weren't touched, when in fact the action was never attempted) is real and user-visible.

Confidence 0.9 rather than higher only because the exact per-path-length crossover (3.5k vs 4k moves) is an estimate, and triggering requires a genuinely large library; but the bug's existence, reachability, and harm are not in doubt.

confirmed.title: Redirect allowlist checks host but not scheme — permits plaintext http:// downgrade redirect
confirmed.description: The custom redirect policy matches only on attempt.url().host_str() (lines 90-98) and never inspects attempt.url().scheme(). reqwest::redirect::Policy is applied to redirect targets regardless of scheme, so a 30x Location: pointing at http://huggingface.co/... (or any allowlisted host over plaintext) passes the host suffix-match and is followed. The doc comment for this very block (lines 72-77) states the allowlist exists because "an on-path attacker could bounce a 302 chain to an off-allowlist host" — but the same on-path attacker can instead inject a 302 to the SAME allowlisted host over http://, downgrading the transfer to cleartext. That defeats TLS confidentiality for the egress this engine is explicitly built to keep private/HTTPS-only. Integrity for the shipped set is still protected by the mandatory SHA256 pins, which is why this is medium rather than high, but the redirect guard's stated purpose (no off-policy egress, including the privacy posture) is partially bypassed, and a non-pinned future entry would also lose integrity. The original request is always https, but redirects are attacker-influenceable on a hostile network, which is the threat model this code already assumes.
confirmed.trigger: On a hostile/on-path network, the attacker returns HTTP 302 Location: http://huggingface.co/<path> for a model GET. host_str() == "huggingface.co" matches REDIRECT_ALLOWED, the closure returns attempt.follow(), and reqwest re-issues the request over plaintext HTTP — exfiltrating which model/file is being fetched in cleartext and exposing the body to tampering (caught only by SHA for pinned files).
confirmed.suggested_fix: In the redirect closure, reject any non-https target before the host check: `if attempt.url().scheme() != "https" { return attempt.stop(); }`. (HF and all allowlisted CDNs serve over https, so this never blocks a legitimate redirect.)

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against downloader.rs:86-100 (redirect closure) and 101-109 (client builder). The closure matches only on attempt.url().host_str() and never inspects attempt.url().scheme(); there is no scheme check anywhere in the closure or builder. The builder (lines 101-109) does NOT call .https_only(true) — in reqwest 0.12 (Cargo.toml:68) https_only defaults to false, and a custom Policy returning attempt.follow() re-issues the request over whatever scheme the Location URL gives. So a 302 Location: http://huggingface.co/<path> has host_str()=="huggingface.co", matches the suffix-match (h == *d), returns attempt.follow(), and reqwest performs a plaintext HTTP GET — a TLS downgrade. The code's own doc comment (lines 72-77) explicitly adopts an on-path attacker who can "bounce a 302 chain" as its threat model, so the scheme-downgrade variant is in-scope, not a hypothetical. Nothing compensates: the source-URL allowlist (registry.rs:567, test all_model_urls_are_huggingface) only checks that the ORIGINAL url starts with https://huggingface.co/ and does nothing for redirect targets — which the downloader comment itself concedes. Harm is genuine: cleartext exposure of which model/file is fetched plus tampering exposure, directly contradicting the engine's stated privacy/HTTPS-only egress posture (lines 8-11). Severity is correctly medium not high because mandatory SHA256 pins (expected_sha256, line 127; check_size_plausible, 141-155) preserve integrity for pinned files even over plaintext — but confidentiality is lost regardless, and a non-pinned entry (expected_sha256: None is a valid variant) loses integrity too. The suggested fix (reject scheme != "https" before the host check) is correct and never blocks a legitimate redirect since all allowlisted hosts serve https. Confidence 0.9 (not higher only because exploitation requires an active on-path MITM, though that is the precondition the code already claims to defend against).

Verified against downloader.rs and registry.rs.

CORE FACTS CONFIRMED:
- downloader.rs lines 86-100: the redirect closure (`Policy::custom`) gates redirect targets ONLY on `attempt.url().host_str()` (lines 90-94, suffix-match against REDIRECT_ALLOWED). A grep for `scheme` across downloader.rs returns ZERO matches — the scheme is never inspected anywhere in the closure or the client builder (lines 101-109). For any allowlisted host the closure returns `attempt.follow()` regardless of scheme.
- reqwest applies this custom policy as the SOLE gate on redirect targets and re-issues the request to whatever `attempt.url()` is; it follows https→http by default unless told otherwise. So a `302 Location: http://huggingface.co/<path>` passes the host check and is followed over cleartext.
- The doc comment at lines 72-77 EXPLICITLY adopts the on-path attacker / 302-chain-manipulation threat model ("an on-path attacker could bounce a 302 chain to an off-allowlist host"). So the threat model that makes redirects attacker-influenceable is one the code itself assumes — not a strawman.
- Original source URLs are all HTTPS (registry.rs lines 86-511), and the registry test `all_model_urls_are_huggingface` (line 567) only enforces `https://huggingface.co/` on the CONFIGURED source URLs — it does nothing about redirect targets.

REACHABILITY (my lens):
- Live, non-dead code: build_shared_client is the only network client; redirect_policy is attached at line 107.
- No upstream guard negates it: the only scheme enforcement (registry test, line 567) covers source URLs, not redirect Location targets. The redirect closure is the only place redirect targets are vetted and it omits scheme.
- Trigger is satisfiable under the code's own adopted threat model: an active on-path attacker / malicious-or-compromised mirror returns a 302 to http://huggingface.co/..., host_str()=="huggingface.co" matches, follow() over plaintext → cleartext egress (which model/file leaks) + body exposed to tampering (caught only by SHA for pinned files).

CAVEAT (why 0.82, not higher): a purely passive on-path attacker cannot forge the http:// 302 through a valid TLS first leg; the downgrade requires the first (HTTPS-reached) server to emit the http redirect (active manipulation, malicious mirror, or compromised endpoint). But the code's stated threat model already assumes exactly this active 302-chain influence, so the guard is incomplete relative to its OWN stated purpose. Severity correctly rated medium: SHA256 pins protect integrity of the shipped set; the confidentiality/privacy downgrade and integrity loss for any future non-pinned entry remain. The suggested fix (`if attempt.url().scheme() != "https" { return attempt.stop(); }` before the host check) is correct and never blocks a legitimate redirect since all allowlisted hosts serve over https.

Verified against platforms/windows/src/engine/src/downloader.rs lines 71-111. The custom redirect policy (Policy::custom, lines 86-100) gates redirects on attempt.previous().len() < 10 (lines 87-88) and a host suffix-match against REDIRECT_ALLOWED (lines 90-98). It never inspects attempt.url().scheme(). In reqwest 0.12 (Cargo.toml line 68), the custom redirect closure is the sole gate on whether a redirect is followed; reqwest does NOT refuse https→http downgrade redirects on its own (it only strips sensitive headers cross-origin). So a 302 with Location: http://huggingface.co/<path> has host_str()=="huggingface.co", matches the allowlist via `h == *d`, returns attempt.follow(), and reqwest re-issues the GET in cleartext HTTP.

Reachability under the code's OWN threat model: the doc comment at lines 72-77 explicitly assumes an on-path attacker who "could bounce a 302 chain to an off-allowlist host." The identical attacker can instead inject a same-host plaintext redirect, which this guard does not stop. The original request is always https (registry URLs all start with https://, asserted by the test at registry.rs:567), but redirects are attacker-controlled in exactly the threat model this code adopts — so the path is reachable, not theoretical.

Impact under the IMPACT lens: the follow-up request goes out over plaintext, leaking which model/file is fetched (URL path) in cleartext and exposing the body to tampering — a TLS-confidentiality downgrade for the egress this module is explicitly built to keep private/HTTPS-only (header lines 8-11). Integrity for the shipped set is recovered by the mandatory SHA256 pin (header line 6/17) + check_size_plausible (lines 141-155), so body tampering of pinned files is caught — correctly making this medium, not high. But confidentiality/the no-off-policy-egress privacy property the guard exists to protect is genuinely (partially) bypassed and is NOT recovered by SHA; a non-pinned/future entry would also lose integrity. The suggested fix (reject scheme != "https" before the host check) is correct and cannot block any legitimate redirect since every allowlisted host serves https. Refutation attempts (reqwest blocks downgrades; original URL is https; needs active MITM) all fail: reqwest does not block the downgrade, the bug concerns attacker-influenced redirect targets, and the on-path attacker is in-scope per the code's own comment. Confidence held at 0.82 rather than higher because the only residual material harm beyond TLS-confidentiality leakage requires a future non-pinned allowlist entry, and the practical exposure is gated on an active on-path adversary.

confirmed.title: Existing-folder routing can emit destinations outside library_root, which apply silently rejects as failed
confirmed.description: folder_prototypes() builds prototype folders from each file's CURRENT parent (f.source.parent()), and semantic_classify routes a matched cluster to proto.path (the existing folder) verbatim. The source files come from the whole `files` table (handle_plan_restructure's PLAN_FILES_SQL selects all non-failed rows regardless of location), while library_root is a separately chosen path. When the scanned corpus includes folders outside the chosen library_root, a cluster can be routed to a prototype whose path is OUTSIDE library_root. At apply time ensure_inside_root() canonicalizes the destination and rejects anything not under canonical_root, so every such move is counted failed with only a debug-level warning and no user-facing reason. The user sees 'N failed' with no explanation, and those files are silently left unsorted. (New-group routes are immune because they always join library_root; only the learn-your-style existing-folder route reuses an arbitrary source parent.)
confirmed.trigger: Scan a corpus spanning multiple roots (e.g. C:\\Photos and D:\\OldPhotos), then run Restructure with library_root = C:\\Photos. A confident cluster matched to a D:\\OldPhotos\\... prototype yields destinations under D:\\, which apply rejects -> failed++ with no surfaced cause.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In folder_prototypes (or semantic_classify), drop any prototype whose path is not under library_root, OR have handle_plan_restructure pass only files located under library_root into the semantic/prototype stage so existing-folder destinations are guaranteed in-root and the plan/apply containment contract holds.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full causal chain by reading the actual source, and the logic is genuinely wrong as described.

1) restructure_semantic.rs:83-105 (folder_prototypes) buckets prototypes by f.source.parent() verbatim, so a prototype's `path` is whatever parent directory the source file currently lives in — anywhere on disk. 2) restructure_semantic.rs:158-176: the existing-folder branch (sim >= FOLDER_MATCH_COS=0.55, line 164) returns proto.path.clone() (line 176) as dest_dir, then line 244 joins the filename. The new-group branch instead uses library_root.join(...) (line 238), so it is immune — exactly as the claim states.

3) The files fed in are NOT filtered to library_root. commands/restructure.rs PLAN_FILES_SQL (lines 23-34) is `FROM files f WHERE f.failed = 0` with no location predicate; semantic_files (lines 173-185) filters only on kind==Image + presence of a CLIP embedding. library_root is a separate IPC param (ipc/mod.rs:252-256) used only as the new-group join base (restructure.rs:189). So out-of-root files reach the prototype/semantic stage and can produce out-of-root prototype destinations.

4) Multi-root accumulation is real, not contrived. StartScan takes a single root_path (ipc/mod.rs:188) but the engine owns a persistent SQLite DB; scan_session.rs:161-228 only builds a per-root incremental SKIP set — it never deletes rows belonging to other roots. Picking a new folder (MainWindow.xaml.cs:451) merely sets FolderPath; no wipe occurs in that path (wipe is a separate explicit action). So scanning C:\Photos then D:\OldPhotos leaves both in `files`.

5) Apply silently rejects. restructure_apply.rs ensure_inside_root (442-452) canonicalizes the dest and bails if !starts_with(canonical_root); the caller (99-103) logs only tracing::warn! and does failed += 1; continue. RestructureApplyResult (214) carries only applied/failed/privilege_error — no per-move reason — so the user sees "N failed" with no explanation and those files stay unsorted. The path-traversal guard prevents actual harm, which is why this is correctly rated low severity; the defect is the silent unexplained failure + non-placement, a real plan/apply containment-contract violation.

Confidence held at 0.82 (not higher) only because the trigger needs the user to have scanned ≥2 distinct roots into one DB AND a confident cluster whose nearest prototype is the out-of-root folder; it is narrow but entirely reachable under normal usage. The finder did not misread the code.

Verified the full chain in source. (1) restructure.rs PLAN_FILES_SQL (lines 23-34) selects every non-failed row in `files` with no root filter. (2) semantic_files (lines 173-185) filters only on FileKind::Image + has-embedding, NOT on library_root. (3) folder_prototypes (restructure_semantic.rs:83-105) builds a prototype keyed on each file's CURRENT parent (f.source.parent()) for any folder with >=4 images, including out-of-root parents. (4) The existing-folder branch (restructure_semantic.rs:164-176) routes a matched cluster to `proto.path.clone()` VERBATIM — no library_root.join — while the new-group branch (line 238) always joins library_root. The claim's scoping (only the learn-your-style route is affected) is exactly right. (5) At apply, ensure_inside_root (restructure_apply.rs:442-452) canonicalizes the dest and bails when not starts_with(canonical_root); the caller (lines 99-103) only emits tracing::warn! and does failed+=1. RestructureApplyResult carries only applied/failed/privilege_error — no per-move reason — so the user sees "N failed" with no cause.

Reachability (my lens) confirmed satisfiable in production: a scan runs against one root (scan.rs:284-287) and NEVER deletes rows from previously-scanned roots. scan_session.rs:171-192 explicitly distinguishes total_files (whole table COUNT) from files_under_root (current-root prefix COUNT) — the engine's own diagnostics acknowledge the table can hold rows outside the current root. The app uses the same AppViewModel.FolderPath for both the scan root and the restructure library_root, so a single-root single-scan session is immune; but scanning C:\Photos then later pointing the sidebar at D:\OldPhotos accumulates both roots' rows in the shared files table. Restructuring with library_root=C:\Photos then sees D:\OldPhotos\... prototypes and can route a confident cross-root cluster (sim>=0.55, FOLDER_MATCH_COS) there → apply rejects → silent failed++.

One inaccuracy: the claim says the rejection logs at "debug-level"; it is actually tracing::warn! (restructure_apply.rs:100). The substantive point (no user-facing reason) holds.

Harm is genuinely low: it requires an uncommon multi-root accumulated DB, a cross-root cluster confident enough to match an out-of-root prototype, and >=4 images in that folder. No data loss/corruption — the apply guard fails safe by design; the defect is a plan/apply containment-contract inconsistency plus a missing user-facing explanation, leaving some files unsorted with an unexplained "failed" count. This matches the stated low / plan-apply-consistency classification. Real and reachable, with bounded cosmetic/UX harm. Confidence is below 0.9 only because the trigger depends on the user re-scanning a second root (rather than clearing/re-picking), which is plausible but not the default flow.

CHAIN VERIFIED:
1. restructure.rs PLAN_FILES_SQL (lines 23-34): selects ALL `files` where `f.failed = 0` — no library_root filter. The semantic embeddings query (lines 130-134) likewise selects all non-failed image rows, no path filter.
2. library_root is a SEPARATE user-chosen path: PlanRestructurePayload.library_root (ipc/mod.rs:256), distinct from the scan's StartScanPayload.root_path (ipc/mod.rs:188). The DB persists across scans of different roots, so the files table can legitimately span multiple roots (e.g. a prior scan of D:\OldPhotos + a later scan of C:\Photos).
3. folder_prototypes (restructure_semantic.rs:83-105) builds a prototype from EACH file's current `f.source.parent()` (min 4 files), regardless of location — so an out-of-root parent folder becomes a prototype.
4. semantic_classify existing-folder branch (line 164-176): when a cluster centroid matches a prototype with sim >= FOLDER_MATCH_COS (0.55), it routes to `proto.path.clone()` VERBATIM. Line 244 then joins the filename → destination under the (possibly out-of-root) prototype folder. The new-group branch (line 238) always joins library_root and is immune, exactly as claimed.
5. apply: ensure_inside_root (restructure_apply.rs:442-452) canonicalizes the dest and bails if it does not start_with(canonical_root). On bail the move is counted failed (line 99-103) with only a tracing::warn! — no per-move reason. RestructureApplyResult (ipc/mod.rs:914-923) carries only applied/failed/privilege_error, so the user sees "N failed" with no surfaced cause.
6. Worse for the in-root members: a content cluster can mix in-root and out-of-root files; if its centroid matches an out-of-root prototype, ALL members get out-of-root destinations. restructure.rs:190-192 builds `moved` from every semantic move (including the doomed ones) and excludes them from rule_files, so those in-root files get NO rule-cascade fallback and are silently left unsorted.

IMPACT LENS: The harm is real and matches the claim, but it is BENIGN in the destructive sense — no data loss/corruption/crash/hang/leak/telemetry. The rejection occurs BEFORE MoveFileExW (lines 99-103), so source files are untouched; nothing is moved or clobbered. The genuine consequence is a plan/apply consistency defect with a poor UX symptom: silent "N failed" with no explanation and affected files left unsorted. That is precisely the low-severity harm the report claims, and it is triggerable with the described multi-root corpus. Confidence 0.8: logic is unambiguous; the residual uncertainty is whether the app UI ever constrains the user to a single scan == library_root in practice (the engine imposes no such constraint, and the IPC contract treats them as independent).

confirmed.title: Server readiness 120 s deadline can be defeated by a stalled /health response
