confirmed.description: The readiness loop polls /health with `client` (line 123), whose only timeout is the 300 s overall request timeout configured at line 110 — there is no per-request or connect timeout. The 120 s deadline check (line 128) sits AFTER the `.send().await`, so if the server accepts the TCP connection but stalls before producing an HTTP response to /health, a single `client.get(&health_url).send().await` can block for up to 300 s, blowing past the intended 120 s readiness budget and the 300 ms loop cadence; the deadline is only re-evaluated once the await returns. Confidence is moderate: modern llama-server normally answers /health quickly (503 while loading, 200 when ready), so this requires an accept-but-no-response stall (e.g. a wedged backend init), which is uncommon but possible.
confirmed.trigger: A llama-server build/backend that binds the port and accepts connections but hangs inside model/backend init before serving /health (e.g. a GPU driver stall). start_with_binary then waits in a single send().await far longer than the advertised 120 s before bailing or falling back to the next binary.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Use a dedicated short per-request timeout for the health probe (e.g. a request_builder().timeout(Duration::from_secs(2)) or a `tokio::time::timeout` around each send()), so each poll fails fast and the 120 s deadline is honored regardless of server-side stalls.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against platforms/windows/src/engine/src/models/vlm_server.rs (start_with_binary, lines 105-133). The readiness loop logic is exactly as claimed:

- Lines 109-112: the `client` is built with ONLY `.timeout(Duration::from_secs(300))`. No `.connect_timeout()` and no per-request `.timeout()` are set. In reqwest 0.12 (Cargo.toml line 68), the builder `.timeout()` is the overall request timeout covering connect + waiting for response headers + body read; there is no separate default read/connect timeout unless explicitly configured. The codebase author clearly knows these are distinct knobs — downloader.rs:103-106 explicitly sets `connect_timeout(30s)` and `read_timeout(60s)`, which are conspicuously absent here.
- Line 118: `deadline = Instant::now() + Duration::from_secs(120)`.
- Line 123: `client.get(&health_url).send().await` uses that 300s client.
- Line 128: the `Instant::now() >= deadline` (120s) check sits AFTER the `.send().await`, so it is only re-evaluated once the await returns.

Re-derived independently: if llama-server binds the port and accepts the TCP connection (connect phase completes quickly) but then stalls before emitting HTTP response headers (wedged backend/model init, e.g. a synchronous GPU driver hang before the listener thread can answer), a single `send().await` can block up to the full 300s. During that block the line-128 deadline is never evaluated and the 300ms loop cadence (line 132) is irrelevant. The intended 120s readiness budget is blown by up to ~180s, and the CUDA→Vulkan per-binary fallback at start()/lines 65-73 is delayed by the same amount.

Reachability confirmed: VlmServer::start is invoked at deep_analyze.rs:341 with no outer tokio::time::timeout wrapper (grep of the engine src shows no timeout around this call), and start() delegates to start_with_binary which runs this exact loop. The finder did not misread the code.

Severity is correctly rated low: the trigger is narrow — modern llama-server normally answers /health quickly (503 while loading, 200 when ready), so it requires a true accept-but-no-HTTP-response stall, which is uncommon. The harm is a bounded UX delay (wait up to ~300s instead of 120s, then bail/fall back) rather than a permanent hang, crash, or data loss; the 300s overall timeout is a backstop. The suggested fix (a short per-poll timeout via request_builder().timeout(Duration::from_secs(2)) or tokio::time::timeout around each send) is appropriate. The bug is genuine, triggerable, and mildly harmful as written — consistent with low severity. Confidence is below 1.0 only because the harmful manifestation depends on a rare server-side stall mode, not because the logic finding itself is in doubt.

Verified against platforms/windows/src/engine/src/models/vlm_server.rs. All cited code facts hold:

- Line 109-112: the readiness `client` is built with ONLY `.timeout(Duration::from_secs(300))`. No `connect_timeout` and no per-request `.timeout()` are set (confirmed: the only `.timeout(` in the file is line 110). In reqwest 0.12, `ClientBuilder::timeout` applies to the entire request including the wait for response headers, which is exactly what `send()` awaits, so this 300 s value bounds the `.send().await`.
- Line 118: `deadline = Instant::now() + Duration::from_secs(120)`.
- Lines 119-133: the loop. Line 123 issues `client.get(&health_url).send().await`; the deadline check (line 128) and the 300 ms cadence sleep (line 132) both sit AFTER that await. So the deadline is only re-evaluated once `send()` returns. A single poll that blocks (server accepted the TCP connection but stalls before emitting any HTTP response) can hang up to 300 s, overshooting the advertised 120 s budget by ~2.5x and ignoring the 300 ms cadence.

Reachability/trigger (the lens): the loop is live, not dead code — it runs on every Deep Analyze that starts the persistent VLM server, which is the preferred/default path (commands/deep_analyze.rs:333-341). The `start` call is awaited directly with no outer `tokio::time::timeout` wrapper, so this loop's own deadline is the sole budget; nothing upstream guards it. The `child.try_wait()` early-exit guard at line 120 only catches a process that EXITED — it does not help a process that is alive but wedged inside model/backend init, which is precisely the trigger.

Why it isn't fully refuted: normal llama-server binds its HTTP listener and answers /health with 503 while loading (the loop's is_success() check exists for exactly that), so the common case responds fast and the bug does NOT fire. The trigger requires an abnormal accept-but-no-response stall (e.g. a GPU driver/init hang that blocks the HTTP accept loop). On this project's 4 GB DirectML / low-VRAM target, a driver stall during backend init is a plausible — if uncommon — production state, so the precondition is satisfiable.

Harm is real but bounded: at worst the readiness/fallback (CUDA→Vulkan, then CLI) is delayed up to 300 s per candidate instead of 120 s; no corruption, leak, or permanent deadlock (the 300 s timeout always eventually fires). This matches the claimed "low" severity. Genuine bug, reachable, mildly harmful — but gated on an uncommon abnormal server state, hence moderate confidence. The suggested fix (a short per-probe timeout via `request_builder().timeout(...)` or `tokio::time::timeout` around each send) is correct.

The code-level claim is technically accurate, but under the IMPACT lens the consequence is benign/recovered, not harmful.

Verified facts in platforms/windows/src/engine/src/models/vlm_server.rs:
- Line 109-112: the client is built with only `.timeout(Duration::from_secs(300))` (reqwest's TOTAL request timeout, covering connect + request + full response). No `.connect_timeout()` and no per-`.send()` override exist.
- Line 118: `let deadline = Instant::now() + Duration::from_secs(120);`
- Line 123: `client.get(&health_url).send().await` — an in-flight poll.
- Line 128: the `Instant::now() >= deadline` check sits AFTER that await, so it can only re-evaluate once `.send()` returns.

So the abstract logic is correct: if the server accepts the TCP connection but stalls before emitting HTTP response headers, a single `.send().await` blocks until the 300 s total timeout, and the 120 s deadline cannot interrupt it. The advertised "120 s readiness budget" can therefore stretch to ~300 s in that narrow stall.

But the harm the IMPACT lens cares about (hang/crash/data-loss/corruption/leak/telemetry) does NOT occur:
1. Not a true hang — the wait is hard-bounded at 300 s by the client timeout; `.send()` always returns or errors.
2. No leak / no orphan — `kill_on_drop(true)` (line 103) plus `child.start_kill()` (line 129) on the deadline path, and the early-exit branch (line 120-121) bails and propagates up through `start()` (lines 65-74) to fail over to the next binary. The process is always reaped.
3. No crash, corruption, data loss, or telemetry.
4. The COMMON loading path is handled correctly: llama-server returns 503 while loading and 200 when ready, so `.send()` returns fast with a non-success status, the deadline is re-checked, it sleeps 300 ms, and re-polls. The flaw only manifests under an uncommon "TCP accept but no HTTP headers" backend-init stall (the trigger itself concedes this).

Net effect: a longer-than-advertised wait (120 s → up to 300 s) before clean fallback/error in a rare scenario — a missed timing budget, fully recovered, matching the claim's own "low severity / moderate confidence". The logic is imperfect but the real-world consequence is benign, so under the IMPACT lens this is refuted (not a harmful bug). The suggested fix (per-probe `tokio::time::timeout` or a 2 s request-builder timeout) is a reasonable hardening, but its absence does not cause the claimed harm.

confirmed.title: CLIP tokenizer skips punctuation pre-tokenization — punctuation glues onto adjacent words, diverging from reference CLIP on the live search path
confirmed.description: Reference openai/CLIP pre-tokenizes with a regex that splits contractions and isolates punctuation runs ('s|'t|...|[\p{L}]+|[\p{N}]|[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]+) before BPE. This port only normalizes (lowercase + whitespace-collapse) and then splits on the ASCII space character (split_words filters empties from s.split(' ')). Punctuation is therefore not separated: a query word like 'dog.' or 'beach,' is byte-encoded as a single unit and BPE-merged with the trailing punctuation glued via the '</w>' end marker, producing a different (and generally worse) token sequence than reference CLIP. encode() is reachable at runtime from commands/embed.rs handle_embed_text_query -> ClipText::embed (free-text semantic search box) and from scene-label prompt embedding, so user queries containing punctuation get a degraded embedding and worse search relevance. The module comment scopes itself as 'minimal' and round-trips the canonical no-punctuation example, so this is a quality regression rather than a crash, but it silently affects any punctuated query.
confirmed.trigger: Type a free-text search query containing attached punctuation, e.g. 'a dog, running.' or 'sunset!'. The comma/period stays glued to 'dog'/'sunset' during tokenization, yielding tokens that differ from reference CLIP and a less accurate query embedding than the same query without punctuation.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a punctuation-isolation step in split_words/normalize that breaks each whitespace token into letter/number runs and standalone punctuation chars (approximating the CLIP regex), then BPE each piece separately — mirroring wordpiece_tokenizer::basic_tokenize's punctuation handling. At minimum, split off leading/trailing ASCII punctuation from each word before the BPE loop.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the logic independently against the actual source.

CODE AS WRITTEN (clip_tokenizer.rs:107-115): `normalize()` only lowercases and collapses whitespace (`split_whitespace().join(" ")`). `split_words()` then splits purely on the ASCII space char (`s.split(' ').filter(|w| !w.is_empty())`). In `encode()` (lines 64-104) each resulting "word" is byte-encoded as one unit and `</w>` is appended only to its LAST char before the BPE merge loop. There is NO punctuation-isolation step anywhere.

REFERENCE CLIP (openai/CLIP SimpleTokenizer): pre-tokenizes with the regex `'s|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d|[\p{L}]+|[\p{N}]|[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]+`, which splits contractions and isolates punctuation runs into their own pieces BEFORE BPE, appending `</w>` per piece. So `"dog, running."` → ["dog", ",", "running", "."] and "dog" becomes the canonical `dog</w>` vocab token.

RE-DERIVED DIVERGENCE: For a query like "a dog, running." the port produces split_words = ["a","dog,","running."]; "dog," byte-encodes to d,o,g,, with `</w>` glued to the comma, so the BPE loop can never form `dog</w>`. The token sequence genuinely differs from reference CLIP and from the same query without punctuation. The finder did NOT misread the code — the claim is logically accurate. The project's own wordpiece_tokenizer.rs basic_tokenize() (lines 92-114) DOES isolate `is_ascii_punctuation()`, confirming the CLIP port is the outlier.

REACHABILITY CONFIRMED: scene_vocab::ENABLE_CLIP = true (scene_vocab.rs:140). commands/embed.rs handle_embed_text_query (line 114) is past the `if !ENABLE_CLIP` guard, calls ClipText::embed (clip_text.rs:58) → tokenizer.encode. So punctuated free-text search queries hit this path at runtime.

HARM / SEVERITY (matches the finder's "low"): This is a quality regression, not a crash. The scene-TAG path is internally self-consistent — the precomputed matrix is generated by gen_scene_matrix.rs using this SAME tokenizer over PROMPT_TEMPLATES that themselves end in "." ("a photo of a {}.", "a {}."), so both sides of the cosine live in the same encoder space; punctuation handling cancels there. The real (modest) impact is only the free-text search box: a punctuated user query yields an embedding that differs from reference CLIP and from the depunctuated query, slightly degrading relevance. CLIP is fairly robust to this, so the effect is marginal — consistent with the claimed "low" severity.

CORROBORATION: DECISIONS.md:2761 explicitly logs "Deferred: CLIP tokenizer reference-regex (#16)" — an acknowledged, deliberately deferred divergence (fixing it requires regenerating the scene matrix and re-tuning SCENE_COSINE_THRESHOLD). Being a known/deferred bug confirms it is real rather than refuting it.

Not docked to refuted because the bug is genuine, triggerable, and harmful (mildly) exactly as described. Confidence 0.9 rather than higher only because the magnitude of search-quality degradation is small and CLIP's robustness limits real-world impact.

VERIFIED REACHABLE AND ACCURATE (low severity, as claimed).

Reachability (my lens): handle_embed_text_query (embed.rs:114) is dispatched from main.rs:623-626 on the EmbedTextQuery IPC command, whose payload.query (ipc/mod.rs:350-351) is arbitrary user free-text from the Library semantic-search box — no upstream punctuation stripping. The early-return guard at embed.rs:121 fires only when !ENABLE_CLIP, but ENABLE_CLIP = true (scene_vocab.rs:140), so the guard does NOT short-circuit; control flows to model.embed(&payload.query) (embed.rs:169) -> ClipText::embed (clip_text.rs:58-59) -> tokenizer.encode (clip_tokenizer.rs:64). The cited path is live in production. The second cited path (scene-label prompt embedding) is also live: ENABLE_CLIP_SCENE_TAGS = true (scene_vocab.rs:146) and SceneLabeler::build embeds PROMPT_TEMPLATES that include a trailing '.' ("a photo of a {}." — scene_vocab.rs:102) through the same encode().

Correctness divergence (re-derived independently): normalize (clip_tokenizer.rs:107-111) only lowercases + collapses whitespace; split_words (clip_tokenizer.rs:113-115) splits solely on the ASCII space char and filters empties. There is NO punctuation-isolation step. Reference openai/CLIP SimpleTokenizer pre-tokenizes with the regex 's|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d|[\p{L}]+|[\p{N}]|[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]+ to separate contractions and punctuation runs before BPE. This port does not, so a token like "dog." is byte-encoded as a single unit (d,o,g,.) and the BPE loop (lines 81-95) appends </w> to the trailing '.', producing a token sequence that differs from reference CLIP (which yields dog</w> and .</w> as distinct tokens). The trigger inputs ('a dog, running.', 'sunset!') would indeed glue punctuation and diverge.

Severity is correctly stated as low: this is a quality regression, not a crash. Punctuation chars exist in the CLIP vocab so they map (the unknown->EOT fallback at line 97 is not hit for common punctuation); an embedding is still produced and search still functions, just with somewhat degraded relevance for punctuated queries versus the same query unpunctuated. The module header (lines 12-15) explicitly scopes itself as 'minimal' and only claims to round-trip the no-punctuation canonical example, confirming this edge was never handled. No tokenizer unit tests assert punctuation handling. Net: a genuine, triggerable, low-severity tokenizer-correctness divergence on the live search path.

Confidence held at 0.82 rather than higher because I did not execute the tokenizer to capture exact token IDs for a punctuated query, and the magnitude of relevance degradation in 512-d space is plausibly small for a single trailing punctuation mark — but the divergence itself and its reachability are both confirmed by source.

The tokenizer divergence is REAL but the bug is BENIGN under the impact lens, and the claim overstates reachability.

CODE VERIFIED (clip_tokenizer.rs): normalize() (lines 107-111) only lowercases + collapses whitespace; split_words() (lines 113-115) splits solely on ASCII space and filters empties; encode() (lines 64-104) byte-encodes each whitespace token as one unit and appends '</w>' to its last char (lines 78-80) before BPE. Reference openai/CLIP pre-tokenizes with the regex 's|'t|...|[\p{L}]+|[\p{N}]|[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]+, isolating punctuation runs/contractions. This port does not. For "sunset!" the '</w>' marker lands on '!' instead of 't', so it even alters which BPE merges fire for the content word, not just the punctuation token. So the divergence from reference CLIP is genuine. The wordpiece_tokenizer (basic_tokenize lines 101-105) does split punctuation, confirming the CLIP port intentionally/accidentally omitted it — but that tokenizer serves a different model family (BGE/GLiNER).

REACHABILITY: Partially as claimed. The free-text search path IS live: handle_embed_text_query (embed.rs:114) -> ClipText::embed (clip_text.rs:58) -> tokenizer.encode, and ENABLE_CLIP=true (scene_vocab.rs:140) so the empty-embedding fast-returns at embed.rs:23/121 do NOT fire. HOWEVER the claimed 'scene-label prompt embedding' path does NOT run the tokenizer at runtime: shared_scene_labeler (scene_vocab.rs:389) calls build_from_default_model (line 269) which loads the precomputed SCENE_EMBEDDINGS static (line 270); the build/embed/embed_batch path is #[allow(dead_code)] (line 193), used only by the offline gen_scene_matrix.rs example. So one of the two cited reach paths is wrong.

IMPACT LENS: No crash/hang/data-loss/corruption/leak/telemetry. The only consequence is a possibly-marginally-worse query embedding for the rare query that contains attached punctuation. CLIP embeddings are robust to small token perturbations — "sunset!" still embeds near "sunset"; downstream is a cosine ranking with an FTS5 fallback (ClipSearchService), so no wrong/empty/failed results, only a tiny ranking nudge. Real search queries seldom carry attached punctuation. The claim self-rates severity 'low'/'tokenizer-correctness' and concedes it 'is a quality regression rather than a crash.' Under the harm-focused verification lens this is a benign/cosmetic fidelity nit, not a triggerable harmful bug, and 'worse search relevance' is asserted but unmeasured. Refuting on impact (benign) plus overstated reach.

confirmed.title: trash_path probes long (>260-char) paths with the verbatim form but deletes with a non-extended path, so the file the probe reports as present cannot actually be trashed
confirmed.description: The existence probe at trash.rs:35-36 uses crate::util::path_safety::to_extended_length(path) (the \\?\ verbatim form) specifically so a >260-char path is classified as present rather than 'already missing' (the #28 fix, whose comment at trash.rs:33-34 states the file is then 'trashed'). But the delete itself (trash.rs:40-44) builds `wide` from path.as_os_str() WITHOUT the extended-length prefix and feeds it to SHCreateItemFromParsingName (trash.rs:59-61). The engine .exe has no longPathAware manifest (documented at util/path_safety.rs:109-110: 'the app's longPathAware doesn't cover this separate .exe'), so for a path between MAX_PATH (260) and the FS limit, SHCreateItemFromParsingName fails. trash_path then returns Err -> trash() records false; in commands/bulk.rs:352-366 existed=true but trashed_ok=false, so the DB row is kept and the file is reported as a failed trash. The outcome is fail-safe (no data loss, no corruption — the row is correctly retained), but it directly contradicts the #28 intent comment that long-path files get trashed: such files can never be sent to the Recycle Bin via this path.
confirmed.trigger: From the Cleanup tab, trash a file whose absolute path exceeds 260 characters on a system without the LongPathsEnabled registry flag. The probe reports it present, SHCreateItemFromParsingName fails on the non-extended path, and the file is reported as a failed delete and left on disk.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the operation long-path-capable rather than only the probe. Because SHCreateItemFromParsingName/IShellItem are shell-namespace APIs that do not reliably honor \\?\ prefixes, the robust fix is to ship a longPathAware manifest with the engine .exe (then pass the normal path); alternatively, document the >260-char limitation explicitly and surface a clearer 'path too long to recycle' message so the #28 comment matches actual behavior.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified every factual element against source, and the probe/delete asymmetry is genuinely present and is the direct, isolated result of the #28 fix.

EVIDENCE (all read directly):
1. trash.rs:35-36 — the existence probe uses crate::util::path_safety::to_extended_length(path) (the \\?\ verbatim form) then std::fs::symlink_metadata. Confirmed.
2. trash.rs:40-44 — the delete builds `wide` from path.as_os_str().encode_wide() with NO \\?\ prefix, and feeds it to SHCreateItemFromParsingName at trash.rs:59-61. Confirmed.
3. git blame proves the asymmetry: the verbatim probe (lines 33-36) was added in commit dafaaf6b (2026-06-01, the "#28 accuracy + residual-bug fixes" sweep), while the delete's non-extended wide-build (lines 40-44) predates it (commit 740149e4, 2026-05-02). The #28 fix touched ONLY the probe, leaving the delete on the bare path. This is exactly the bug shape claimed.
4. The engine crate has NO manifest/.rc/build.rs (only Cargo.toml + src), so the engine .exe is not longPathAware. The longPathAware manifest lives in FileID.App/app.manifest:43 (the WinUI app, a separate process). path_safety.rs:108-110 explicitly documents "the app's longPathAware doesn't cover this separate .exe" and "the system LongPathsEnabled registry flag is off by default, so std::fs / jwalk silently fail on deep paths unless we prefix explicitly." This is the project's own confirmation that non-prefixed FS access fails past MAX_PATH for this process.
5. The #28 intent that long-path files are trashed is stated at bulk.rs:331-332: "classified as present (and trashed) instead of 'already missing' (#28)." (The claim cited trash.rs:33-34 for the word "trashed"; it is actually in bulk.rs:332 — a minor location error, but the substance is correct: trash.rs:33-34 says the probe exists so as not to "silently no-op the delete," same intent.)
6. Caller bulk.rs:352-386: existed=true + trashed_ok=false → no DELETE FROM files (row retained), reported as "trash failed." Confirmed fail-safe — no data loss/corruption; the row is correctly kept.

CORE TECHNICAL PREMISE — confirmed sound: SHCreateItemFromParsingName is a shell-namespace parsing API (IShellFolder::ParseDisplayName). The Windows shell is not long-path aware and, in a process without longPathAware and with LongPathsEnabled off (default), path parsing enforces MAX_PATH (260). A file between 260 chars and the FS limit exists on disk only because the verbatim discovery walk indexed it; the non-extended path handed to SHCreateItemFromParsingName cannot be parsed → error → trash_path returns Err → trash() records false. The probe (extended-length) succeeds while the delete (non-extended) fails for exactly this path-length window. The sibling shell site thumbnail.rs:64-68 also uses the bare path, consistent with the shell not honoring \\?\ — which is why the claim's own suggested_fix correctly notes that prefixing would not reliably help and a manifest is the real fix.

CONCLUSION: The logic is genuinely wrong as written and triggerable as described — for a >260-char path on a default-registry system, the file is reported as a failed trash and left on disk, contradicting the #28 "(and trashed)" intent; such files can never reach the Recycle Bin via this path. Harm is correctly characterized as LOW/benign: fail-safe (DB row retained, no data loss), the only user-visible effect being a "trash failed" message and the unreachable #28 goal. Confidence is below certainty only because triggering requires the specific combination of a >260-char absolute path AND LongPathsEnabled disabled, and the harm is minor — but the defect itself is real, not a misread.

Code facts verified: the existence probe (trash.rs:35-36) uses the verbatim \\?\ form via to_extended_length while the delete (trash.rs:40-44) builds `wide` from plain path.as_os_str() and feeds SHCreateItemFromParsingName (trash.rs:59-61); the engine .exe has no longPathAware manifest (path_safety.rs:108-110); and bulk.rs:352-386 keeps the DB row and reports "trash failed" when existed=true but trashed_ok=false. So the probe/delete path-form mismatch and the downstream reporting are factually accurate.

But the claim is REFUTED as a real, harmful bug under the trigger/reachability lens for three reasons:

1. The load-bearing premise — that SHCreateItemFromParsingName FAILS on a plain >260-char path and would SUCCEED with a \\?\ prefix — is asserted without proof and is internally contradicted by the claim's own suggested fix ("shell-namespace APIs do not reliably honor \\?\ prefixes"). SHCreateItemFromParsingName is a shell-namespace parsing API, not a raw Win32 file function (CreateFileW/MoveFileExW); its MAX_PATH behavior is not the same as the file APIs the \\?\ prefix is designed for. If the shell API doesn't honor \\?\, then prefixing the delete (what the title implies) would not be a valid fix at all.

2. The codebase deliberately and consistently distinguishes the two API families: raw file APIs get to_extended_length (move_file at restructure_apply.rs:234-235, plus discovery/dbwriter/platform per the comment), while BOTH shell-parsing call sites — trash.rs:40 and thumbnail.rs:64 — intentionally pass the plain path. The non-prefixing in trash.rs is the established pattern, not an oversight. The probe needs the prefix because std::fs::symlink_metadata is a raw file API; the shell delete legitimately uses the plain path. There is no inconsistency that causes a failure.

3. The outcome is explicitly fail-safe (claim concedes: no data loss, no corruption). bulk.rs:379-386 retains the row and reports "trash failed" — the correct behavior if the OS/shell genuinely cannot recycle that path. The #28 fix achieved its real goal (stop the silent false-success / silent no-op that deleted the row while the file remained); the "(and trashed)" comment is at most aspirational shorthand. Worst case here is a slightly optimistic comment plus an honest "trash failed" report — a cosmetic/doc nit, not a triggerable correctness bug. Severity is self-rated "low" and the trigger is narrow (real >260-char path AND LongPathsEnabled off), and on such a system reporting a failed trash and leaving the file in place is arguably the correct result. Confidence held below 0.85 because I could not independently run the exact SHCreateItemFromParsingName behavior on a >260-char path on a non-LongPathsEnabled box; but the claim bears the burden of proving harm and does not, and its self-contradiction plus the fail-safe outcome make "real" unsupportable.

Verified against the actual source. The asymmetry the claim describes is real and present in trash.rs:

1) Existence probe (trash.rs:35-36) uses crate::util::path_safety::to_extended_length(path) — the verbatim \\?\ form — for std::fs::symlink_metadata, so a path >260 chars is correctly classified as PRESENT. The same long-path-aware probe is duplicated at the call site bulk.rs:333-336 to set `existed=true`.

2) The actual delete (trash.rs:40-44) builds `wide` from path.as_os_str().encode_wide() WITHOUT the extended-length prefix, and feeds that bare path to SHCreateItemFromParsingName(PCWSTR(wide.as_ptr()), None) at trash.rs:59-61.

3) path_safety.rs:107-114 documents (and is the basis for the claim) that the engine .exe has no longPathAware manifest and LongPathsEnabled is off by default, so Win32 file APIs fail on >260-char paths unless the \\?\ prefix is applied. Shell parsing-name APIs (SHCreateItemFromParsingName/IShellItem) do not reliably honor \\?\, so for a path between MAX_PATH and the FS limit the call returns an error HRESULT, .context()? makes trash_path return Err, and trash() records false.

4) Caller behavior confirmed at bulk.rs:352-387: with existed=true and trashed_ok=false, the DELETE FROM files is NOT run (the row is kept) and the item is pushed as ok=false "trash failed". So such a file can never be sent to the Recycle Bin through this path, which directly contradicts the optimistic parenthetical "(and trashed)" in the #28 comments (trash.rs:33-34, bulk.rs:331-332).

So the mechanism is genuine and triggerable in the stated narrow scenario (path length between 260 and the FS limit, LongPathsEnabled off, file present so it was discovered by the verbatim-aware walk).

IMPACT lens: the harm is fail-safe and bounded — NO data loss (file stays on disk), NO DB corruption (the row is correctly retained, consistent with the file still existing), NO crash/hang/leak/telemetry (clean Err path). The narrow #28 goal of not SILENTLY no-opping is actually met: the delete is attempted and the failure is honestly reported. The real consequence is a functional shortfall plus a misleading comment: a long-path file the user asked to trash is reported "trash failed" and left on disk rather than being recycled. That is a genuine, low-severity correctness bug (correctly tagged severity=low), so I classify it real, but the impact is benign/recoverable rather than dangerous — confidence is moderate because the only "harm" is the inability to recycle these edge-case files plus the inaccurate comment, not any of the higher-severity outcomes the lens screens for.

confirmed.title: Parallel→simple fallback orphans up to 12 stale .part-NN files (disk leak)
confirmed.description: download_parallel writes per-range chunks to `<dest>.part-00`..`.part-11` (part_file_path), while download_simple uses a different single tmp path `<dest>.<ext>.part`. The fallback at line 474-476 (`if total < MIN_BYTES_FOR_PARALLEL || !supports_ranges`) hands off to download_simple and never deletes any pre-existing `.part-NN` files. If a previous attempt ran the parallel path far enough to create per-range part files (large file, ranges supported) and a later attempt takes the simple path instead — e.g. the server now omits Accept-Ranges and the one-byte range probe also fails/returns 200, or the remote file shrank below the 5 MB MIN_BYTES_FOR_PARALLEL threshold across a revision — those up-to-12 `.part-NN` files (each potentially hundreds of MB for a multi-GB GGUF) are left on disk indefinitely. The parallel path only cleans `.part-NN` on its OWN success/SHA-fail/size-fail (lines 603-605, 617-619, 627-629), never on the simple-path branch. On a 4 GB-class target this stranded space can matter.
confirmed.trigger: Attempt 1: download_parallel streams several `<dest>.part-NN` files for a large range-supporting URL, then fails (network drop) leaving the parts on disk by design. Attempt 2 (Retry): the CDN now returns a HEAD/probe without Accept-Ranges and a 200 on the range probe (or the registry/remote size is now < 5 MB), so download_parallel falls through to download_simple at line 475. download_simple completes via `<dest>.<ext>.part` and renames; the original `.part-00..NN` files are never removed.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Before delegating at line 475, best-effort remove the per-range parts: `for i in 0..PARALLEL_PARTS { let _ = tokio::fs::remove_file(part_file_path(&request.destination, i)).await; }` (the simple path can't resume from them anyway, so they are pure garbage at that point).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against downloader.rs and its caller prewarm.rs.

FACTS (all confirmed in source):
- Fallback at lines 474-476: `if total < MIN_BYTES_FOR_PARALLEL || !supports_ranges { return download_simple(client.clone(), request, cancel.clone(), |p| progress(p)).await; }` delegates to download_simple and returns WITHOUT removing any pre-existing per-range parts.
- Path naming is genuinely distinct: `part_file_path` (lines 674-678) yields `<file_name>.part-00`..`.part-11` (it uses `dest.file_name()`, the full filename, despite the `stem` var name). download_simple's tmp and download_parallel's combined tmp both use `dest.with_extension("<ext>.part")` => `<file_name>.part` (lines 185-190, 561-566). So `.part-NN` and `.part` never collide; the simple path cannot consume or overwrite the per-range parts.
- The ONLY `.part-NN` cleanup sites are lines 603-605 (SHA fail), 617-619 (size fail), 627-629 (success) — all inside the parallel branch AFTER the line-475 fallback return, so unreachable once the fallback fires.
- No sweeping/orphan cleanup exists anywhere: prewarm.rs only removes the zip-extract dest (line 462) and sentinel .tmp (503/519); wipe.rs removes user files. Grep of the whole engine src for `part-`/`.part`/`remove_file` confirms no model-cache part sweep.
- Parts survive failure/cancellation by design (header comment line 20, doc line 423), and retry reuses the same stable `file.dest` from `registry::lookup_full` (prewarm.rs 313-318), so stale parts persist across attempts on the same destination.

REACHABILITY: Requires attempt 1 to run the parallel path (total>=5MB AND supports_ranges) far enough to create parts, then fail; and attempt 2 to take the fallback — which on a same-server retry needs the CDN to both omit Accept-Ranges on HEAD (line 462 false) AND answer 200 not 206 to the 0-0 probe (lines 465-471 not returning `(>=5MB, true)`), OR the remote file to shrink below 5MB. Uncommon but genuinely possible (HF CDN edge variance; model re-uploads). Low probability, not zero.

HARM: Up to 12 `.part-NN` files, each potentially hundreds of MB for a multi-GB GGUF, stranded indefinitely with no reclamation path — a real disk leak on a 4GB-class target. Matches the stated "low" severity. The asymmetry (every other parallel-path exit cleans parts, the fallback alone does not) confirms an oversight rather than intent; the suggested best-effort remove loop is safe and consistent with existing code. Bug is genuine, triggerable, and harmful. Confidence held below ceiling only because the trigger relies on relatively uncommon server-behavior changes across attempts.

Verified against downloader.rs and prewarm.rs. The code structure is incontrovertible: download_parallel at downloader.rs:474-476 (`if total < MIN_BYTES_FOR_PARALLEL || !supports_ranges { return download_simple(...) }`) delegates to download_simple WITHOUT first removing any pre-existing `<stem>.part-NN` files. The ONLY `.part-NN` cleanup sites are inside the parallel path's own success/SHA-fail/size-fail branches (lines 626-629, 603-605, 617-619) plus the oversized-stale-part discard in download_range_with_retry (line 722). The simple path (171-402) operates exclusively on a distinct tmp filename `<dest>.<ext>.part` (lines 185-190, renamed at 386), e.g. `model.gguf.part`, which never collides with `model.gguf.part-00..11` (part_file_path, lines 674-678) — so it neither consumes nor deletes the orphans. I also confirmed no upstream cleanup: prewarm.rs has zero `.part-NN` removal (its line 462 `remove_file(&file.dest)` is the post-zip-extract cleanup, unrelated).

Trigger/reachability lens: Attempt-1 part-file creation is routine — every default model (RAM++ Swin-L ONNX, CLIP, multi-GB GGUF VLMs) is a large HF-CDN file that takes the parallel path (lines 462-472), and on network-drop failure the parts are intentionally left for resume (header line 20; lines 626-629 "OK to leave them on failure"). For attempt 2 to hit the simple fallback on the SAME stable destination/URL (confirmed stable across retries via prewarm.rs DownloadRequest at 313-318/411-416), ranges must flip unsupported: HEAD omits Accept-Ranges AND the one-byte 0-0 range probe returns 200/None (probe_range_support, 646-672). The downloader's own comments (lines 441-443) document that HF's CDN is inconsistent about advertising ranges — that flakiness is why the probe exists — but the probe is a backstop, so the simple fallback only fires when the edge genuinely won't honor `Range:` on GET (transient/uncommon), or if the registry size drops < 5 MB across a revision. So the trigger is satisfiable but narrow.

Harm: if the simple-path attempt 2 then succeeds, the install sentinel is written (prewarm.rs 480-533) and prewarm never re-runs for that model, so no later parallel attempt ever cleans the orphans → they persist indefinitely. Up to 12 parts (~170 MB each per the code's own comment at line 571) is meaningful stranded space on the documented 4 GB-class target. Severity "low" is accurate, and the suggested fix (best-effort remove_file of the 12 part paths before delegating at line 475) is correct and mirrors existing patterns. Genuine, triggerable, and harmful — confidence held below high only because the precondition (CDN flipping range support across retries, then the simple path succeeding) is uncommon rather than routine.

Verified against platforms/windows/src/engine/src/downloader.rs.

LOGIC GAP CONFIRMED (incontrovertible from source):
- The two paths use DIFFERENT tmp names. Parallel parts: part_file_path (lines 674-678) = `<parent>/<filename>.part-00..11` (e.g. `model.gguf.part-00`). Simple tmp (lines 185-190) = `dest.with_extension("<ext>.part")` = `model.gguf.part`. They are distinct files; download_simple cannot consume or overwrite the `.part-NN` files.
- The parallel path leaves `.part-NN` on failure/cancel BY DESIGN (lines 20, 423, 626 "OK to leave them on failure") for resume.
- `.part-NN` cleanup exists ONLY on the parallel path's own outcomes: SHA-fail (603-605), size-fail (617-619), success-after-rename (627-629). The fallback at line 474-476 (`if total < MIN_BYTES_FOR_PARALLEL || !supports_ranges { return download_simple(...) }`) delegates immediately with NO cleanup loop. So pre-existing `.part-NN` files are orphaned when the next attempt takes the simple branch.

REACHABILITY: prewarm.rs (lines 313-325, 411-418) calls download_parallel with a stable dest from the registry; on failure the app surfaces a Retry (lines 371-382, message even promises "Downloads resume from where they stopped"). A retry re-enters download_parallel against the same dest, re-deriving total/supports_ranges from a fresh HEAD + range probe each time (lines 444-472). So if attempt 1 created parts then failed, and attempt 2's probe now yields !supports_ranges or total<5MB, the fallback fires and orphans the parts. This is a genuine, reachable code path.

IMPACT (the lens): Real disk leak. If attempt 2's simple path then succeeds, it renames `model.gguf.part`→`model.gguf` and the up-to-12 `.part-NN` files persist with no later code referencing them (the file is complete; no further attempt). On the range-support-flip sub-case the file is still large, so stranded parts can total ~a full model's worth of bytes — meaningful on a 4 GB-class target. Not masked, not recovered, not cosmetic.

Why confidence is 0.68, not higher: the TRIGGER is genuinely uncommon. It requires a server that advertised/honored ranges in attempt 1 to flip to no-ranges (HEAD omits Accept-Ranges AND the 0-0 range probe returns 200 not 206) in attempt 2, or a same-URL/same-dest remote revision shrinking a multi-GB file below 5 MB. HF's CDN is consistent about range support per object, so the large-stranded-bytes scenario is rare. Severity "low" is accurate. The logic gap is certain; the harm materializes only under an unusual server-behavior change, but when it does it is a real, persistent leak. The suggested fix (best-effort remove `.part-NN` before delegating at line 475) is correct and cheap since the simple path can't resume from them anyway.

confirmed.title: wipeLibrary does not interlock against in-flight bulk person/tag handlers, allowing post-wipe phantom rows
confirmed.description: handle_wipe_library waits (bounded) only for the in-flight scan (scan_state) and the in-flight face-clustering pass (face_cluster_active) before truncating. It does NOT wait for the other spawned writer handlers that hold their own db Arc clone and can commit AFTER the wipe: handle_merge_clusters / handle_revert_merge / handle_rename_person / handle_mark_persons_as_unknown / handle_apply_tags / handle_restore_from_trash and the restructure handlers. wipe_all serializes on the writer mutex (no corruption), but a mergeClusters or revertMerge that acquires the lock after wipe_all completes re-INSERTs persons rows (revertMerge does INSERT OR IGNORE INTO persons; mergeClusters recomputes/UPSERTs persons) and reassigns face_prints — repopulating the just-wiped DB with ghost People cards, exactly the data-class the (already-fixed) wipe↔face-clustering interlock was added to prevent, just via a different command. Restore-from-trash can likewise re-INSERT files rows after the wipe. Today the app serializes these in the UI, but the module doc explicitly states the engine is the sole DB owner and must enforce the interlock 'regardless of what the app sends', which this path does not.
confirmed.trigger: Dispatch wipeLibrary while a mergeClusters/revertMerge/restoreFromTrash handler is in flight (e.g. user clicks Wipe in Settings right after confirming a person merge): the bulk handler's transaction commits after wipe_all truncates, leaving phantom persons/files rows in a library that reported 'wiped'.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Extend the engine-side interlock to cover all writer handlers — e.g. gate every db-writing handler behind a shared in-flight counter / single-writer epoch and have handle_wipe_library wait for it to drain (the same bounded-wait pattern already used for scan_state and face_cluster_active) before calling db::wipe_all, or take an exclusive 'wipe in progress' flag the writer handlers check on entry.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading the cited file and all referenced handlers/dispatch. The claim holds as written.

EVIDENCE:
1. wipe.rs:28-51 — handle_wipe_library waits (bounded) ONLY on scan_state (lines 28-35) and face_cluster_active (lines 47-51) before calling db::wipe_all. No other handler is gated.
2. main.rs dispatch — the other writer handlers are async-spawned with their own db.clone() and NO shared in-flight/epoch gate: MergeClusters (612-622), ApplyTags (579-589), RenamePerson (629-639), MarkPersonsAsUnknown (640-650), RestoreFromTrash (757-767), RevertMerge (768-778), Plan/ApplyRestructure (527-548). Only scan_state, deep_analyze_active, and face_cluster_active have coordination; the bulk/trash/restructure paths have none. Grep of bulk.rs found no epoch/in_flight/drain/wipe coordination.
3. The handlers verifiably re-INSERT/UPSERT into tables that wipe_all truncates: trash.rs:87-99 INSERT OR IGNORE INTO files; trash.rs:203-206 INSERT OR IGNORE INTO persons + face_prints reassignment (212-217); bulk.rs:136 INSERT OR REPLACE INTO tags; bulk.rs:464/468 persons UPSERT/recompute; restructure_apply.rs:557 INSERT INTO files.
4. db/mod.rs:142-182 wipe_all is a single unchecked_transaction DELETE-all on the writer connection. Since each task acquires the same parking_lot::Mutex, there is NO corruption — but there is NO ordering guarantee. A bulk/trash task that was spawned before the wipe but acquires the writer lock AFTER wipe_all's spawn_blocking releases it will commit phantom rows into the just-wiped DB.

LOGIC RE-DERIVATION: tokio reads stdin serially but executes handlers concurrently on separate tasks. A mergeClusters/revertMerge/restoreFromTrash dispatched just before wipeLibrary produces two concurrent tasks; wipe does not wait for the bulk task, so the lock-acquisition order is a race. The losing-order interleaving leaves phantom persons/files/tags rows after a library that reported "wiped" — the exact data-class the (already-shipped) wipe<->face-clustering interlock was added to prevent (see wipe.rs:37-46), reachable via a different command. The finder did not misread: the gating set is genuinely incomplete.

CONTRACT POINT: wipe.rs:26-27 and 45-46 explicitly state the engine is the sole DB owner and must "enforce this interlock here regardless of what the app sends." The bulk/trash/restructure paths violate that documented invariant — so even though the app serializes these in the UI today (the finder concedes this), the engine-side guarantee the module claims is not met.

SEVERITY/CAVEATS supporting "low" and confidence < 0.9: (a) No data corruption — mutex serializes writes. (b) In practice the WinUI app serializes Wipe vs bulk actions, so triggering requires concurrent dispatch the UI doesn't normally allow; this is a contract/defense-in-depth gap more than a user-observable bug today. (c) The race window is narrow (the bulk tx must commit after wipe_all). These keep it from being incontrovertibly high-impact, but the code-level defect — incomplete interlock contradicting the file's own documented invariant, with concrete re-INSERT paths — is genuine and triggerable.

VERIFIED REAL (low severity, as claimed). The cited path is reachable and the harm is genuine.

Dispatch model (main.rs:329-393, 467-827): the stdio loop reads ONE command at a time, but every writer handler is tokio::spawn'd — MergeClusters (612-622), RevertMerge (768-778), RestoreFromTrash (757-767), RenamePerson (629-639), ApplyTags (579-589), WipeLibrary (807-819). So handler N runs concurrently with the dispatch of N+1. Two commands sent back-to-back over stdin therefore execute concurrently in the engine; the app's _writeLock (EngineClient.cs:840-848) only serializes the stdin byte-write to prevent torn frames, NOT engine-side handler completion.

wipe.rs:28-51: handle_wipe_library waits (bounded, 100×50ms) ONLY on scan_state and face_cluster_active before truncating. There is no shared in-flight counter/epoch covering the bulk/trash handlers — confirmed by reading the full file (lines 1-98).

Re-insert is real after the wipe:
- handle_revert_merge (trash.rs:203-211): `INSERT OR IGNORE INTO persons ...` then `UPDATE face_prints SET person_id`.
- handle_merge_clusters (bulk.rs:446-475): UPDATE/DELETE/UPSERT persons + representative_face_id.
- handle_restore_from_trash (trash.rs:87-99): `INSERT OR IGNORE INTO files ...`.
Each handler does its write inside its own tokio::task::spawn_blocking taking db.lock() (bulk.rs:444, trash.rs:29/196, wipe.rs:53-54).

Timing window: db is a parking_lot::Mutex (NOT FIFO/fair). wipe_all (db/mod.rs:142-198) truncates every data table + FTS in one transaction, commits (line 187), then checkpoints+VACUUMs. If wipe's spawn_blocking grabs db.lock() before the merge/revert blocking task is scheduled onto the blocking pool (plausible — the bulk handler's blocking closure may not yet be running, and the lock is unfair), the wipe commits first; merge/revert then acquires the lock and re-INSERTs persons/files rows into the just-wiped DB → phantom People cards / files rows in a library that reported "wiped" via LibraryWiped{ok:true}. This is exactly the data class the existing face-clustering interlock (wipe.rs:37-51) was added to prevent, via a different command.

wipe.rs:27 explicitly states the engine is the sole DB owner and must enforce the interlock "regardless of what the app sends," so relying on app-side UI serialization is not the engine's contract; WipeLibraryAsync (EngineClient.cs:301-302) is documented fire-and-forget with no in-flight-operation gate.

Why not higher confidence / why low severity is correct: triggering requires precise interleaving of two relatively rare user actions (confirm a person merge / restore-from-trash, then immediately click Wipe), and in the common case the app's UI flow awaits the bulk reply before offering wipe, plus wipe_all serializes on the same mutex so there is NO corruption — only logically-stale ghost rows that a subsequent rescan/wipe would clear. The fix suggestion (extend the bounded-wait interlock to a shared in-flight writer counter) is sound and matches the existing pattern. Genuine, triggerable, harmful-but-minor → real, low.

The claim's mechanism is partly correct but its enumeration of repopulating handlers and its harm are wrong, and the one genuine repopulation is benign under the IMPACT lens.

VERIFIED CORRECT (mechanism): The dispatch loop (main.rs:329-393) reads commands serially but tokio::spawns each writer handler; wipe (main.rs:807-819) and revertMerge (main.rs:768-778) both do their DB work in spawn_blocking contending for the single writer mutex. wipe.rs:28-51 interlocks ONLY scan_state and face_cluster_active, not the bulk/trash handlers. So a wipe_all that acquires the mutex first, then a handler that acquires it after, can commit into the wiped DB. The race window is real and reachable.

BUT the claimed repopulators mostly don't repopulate:
- mergeClusters (bulk.rs:446-475): only UPDATE face_prints + DELETE/UPDATE persons — NO INSERT or UPSERT. Claim that it 'recomputes/UPSERTs persons' is false. Post-wipe it touches 0 rows.
- renamePerson (bulk.rs:549) / markPersonsAsUnknown (bulk.rs:586) / applyTags (gated on a files SELECT that fails post-wipe): pure UPDATEs, no repopulation.
- restructure handlers (restructure_apply.rs:324): UPDATE files only; the INSERT INTO files at line 557 is a #[cfg(test)] fixture.
- restoreFromTrash (trash.rs:87): INSERT OR IGNORE INTO files is gated by the SEC-7 containment check (trash.rs:39-57) reading scan_sessions.root_path; post-wipe scan_sessions is empty so every item is refused and no row is inserted. The handler holds the lock for its entire body, so it runs strictly before or after the wipe — if after, containment blocks it.
- revertMerge (trash.rs:204): the ONLY genuine repopulator — INSERT OR IGNORE INTO persons is unconditional, so post-wipe it lands one phantom persons row.

IMPACT of the one real repopulation: the phantom row has file_count=0, name=NULL, is_unknown=0, and zero face_prints (its UPDATE face_prints WHERE id=... hit 0 rows because faces were wiped). It is invisible to every read surface: People cards (PeopleViewModel.cs:180-183) use INNER JOIN face_prints + WHERE is_unknown=0, so a faceless person produces NO card; findMergeSuggestions (bulk.rs:703-705) JOINs face_prints; restructure (restructure.rs:28-32) JOINs face_prints and requires a name; clustering name-carry (face_clustering.rs:133-134) requires name/is_unknown. Moreover the next clustering pass runs DELETE FROM persons (face_clustering.rs:293), erasing the orphan. So the specifically-claimed harm — 'phantom/ghost People cards after a wipe that reported success' — does not occur. The leftover is a non-surfacing, self-healing orphan row, not a visible ghost card and not data the user perceives.

Net: the harm is cosmetic-at-most (one invisible orphan persons row in a window that the app already serializes), nowhere near the data-class the scan/face-clustering interlocks prevent (those leave VISIBLE half-populated libraries / ghost People cards because they re-INSERT named/faced persons or files). The architectural-purity point ('engine should enforce regardless of app') is fair as a hardening suggestion, but under the IMPACT lens this is benign, not a real harmful bug. Confidence held below 0.9 because the engine-owner invariant is genuinely violated and a future read path keying off bare persons rows could surface the orphan, so it's a legitimate low-value hardening item — just not the harmful bug as described.

contested.title: RAM++ batch coordinator panics the engine on thread-spawn failure instead of degrading gracefully
contested.description: spawn() uses `.expect("spawn fileid-ramplus-batch thread")` on the OS thread builder. If the OS refuses a new thread (handle/memory pressure on a very large library), this panics. The panic occurs inside ModelStack::load_default during scan setup and is not caught, so it aborts the engine process. This contradicts the documented graceful-degrade pattern used by the analogous CLIP batch coordinator (batch_clip.rs:92-104 logs an error and disables the stage instead of panicking) and by the decoder-pool spawn (tagging.rs:722-730) and the model-pool stagger (load_pool). The RAM++ batch path is opt-in (FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE>1), so the blast radius is narrow, but a thread-exhaustion condition that the rest of the pipeline survives will here kill the whole engine.
contested.trigger: Set FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE>1 with a dynamic-batch RAM++ ONNX installed, then start a scan under OS thread/handle exhaustion (very large library, many concurrent decoder/worker threads). The thread spawn fails and the .expect() panics, aborting the engine instead of falling back to no-RAM++-tagging.
contested.suggested_fix: Mirror ClipBatchCoordinator::spawn: match on the Builder::spawn result, log tracing::error! on failure and return the Arc with a sender whose receiver is dropped (so tag() returns the graceful 'channel closed' Err per request and tagging falls back to CLIP scene-tags), rather than `.expect()`.

verdicts.reasoning:
The cited code is real: ram_plus_batch.rs:106-109 uses `.expect("spawn fileid-ramplus-batch thread")` on the thread Builder, and the analogous coordinators do degrade instead (the comparison file is actually pipeline/batch_clip.rs:92-104, NOT models/batch_clip.rs as claimed; tagging.rs:719-730 also logs-and-continues). So there IS a stylistic inconsistency and a real panic path on thread-spawn failure.

BUT the claim's central harm assertion — "the panic ... is not caught, so it aborts the engine process" / "will here kill the whole engine" — is FALSE, refuted by three independent facts:

1. Cargo.toml:239-245 deliberately sets `panic = "unwind"` (NOT abort), with an explicit comment that this exists so a handler panic unwinds only its task and "the engine keeps serving other commands ... Do not re-add panic = abort."

2. scan.rs:161-188: `ModelStack::load_default` (which calls RamPlusBatchCoordinator::spawn at tagging.rs:541) runs inside `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`. Under unwind, a panic in that closure is captured by tokio as a JoinError. The match arm `Ok(Err(err))` at scan.rs:169 handles exactly this case — its tracing message literally reads "model stack load panicked" — emitting a graceful `model_load_failed` IPC error, setting ScanPhase::Failed, clearing scan state, and returning. The engine process survives and continues serving IPC.

3. logging.rs:51-77: the panic hook explicitly "leaves default unwinding behavior intact; it just forces a tracing::error first." It does not abort.

Therefore a thread-spawn failure at line 109 produces a clean scan-level failure with a user-facing error, not an engine abort. The worst-case outcome is essentially the same graceful degradation the claim demands (scan fails cleanly vs. silently falling back to CLIP scene-tags). The remaining delta — this opt-in path fails the whole scan rather than transparently falling back per-request — is a minor UX nit on an explicitly discouraged, opt-in (FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE>1) path, NOT the "aborts the engine process" harm the claim asserts. The bug as described is not real.

Verified against source. ram_plus_batch.rs:106-109 spawns the coordinator thread via std::thread::Builder::new().name("fileid-ramplus-batch").spawn(...).expect("spawn fileid-ramplus-batch thread") — a panic on OS thread-spawn failure, exactly as claimed. The analogous graceful-degrade pattern is confirmed (the claim cited models/batch_clip.rs but the real file is pipeline/batch_clip.rs:87-104): ClipBatchCoordinator::spawn match-es on Builder::spawn(), logs tracing::error! on failure ("CLIP embeddings disabled for this scan"), and still returns the Arc so embed() yields the graceful "channel closed" Err per request. Its comment explicitly states the intent: "Don't panic mid-scan if the OS refuses a new thread (handle/memory pressure on a very large library)." So the RAM++ path deliberately diverges from the codebase's own documented robustness contract. Reachability confirmed at tagging.rs:533-555: spawn() is called inside load_default, gated on FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE>1 (an opt-in tuning knob) + a dynamic-batch ONNX present + a successful RamPlusTagger::load — all satisfiable in production. Notably, every sibling failure branch there (load error, ONNX missing, tags-path unresolved) degrades to (None, None); only this spawn .expect() panics. The trigger (thread/handle exhaustion on a very large library) is rare but real and is precisely the condition the CLIP coordinator was hardened against. Minor claim inaccuracies: wrong path for the CLIP analog (cosmetic) and the "aborts the engine process" framing is strategy-dependent (a panic here unwinds; it only hard-aborts under panic=abort). Severity 'low' is appropriate given the opt-in gate. Core defect is genuine, reachable, and harmful.

The `.expect("spawn fileid-ramplus-batch thread")` at ram_plus_batch.rs:109 does exist and is genuinely inconsistent with the graceful-degrade pattern used by the analogous coordinators (ClipBatchCoordinator::spawn at pipeline/batch_clip.rs:92-104 matches on the Builder::spawn Result, logs tracing::error! and returns degraded; the decoder pool at tagging.rs:719-730 logs tracing::warn! and continues with fewer threads). So the suggested fix is a legitimate code-quality cleanup.

However, the claim's defining HARM — "the panic ... is not caught, so it aborts the engine process" / "kill the whole engine" — is FALSE under the IMPACT lens, contradicted by two independent facts in the actual source:

1) The engine deliberately uses panic = "unwind", NOT abort (Cargo.toml:234-245; all release profiles inherit from [profile.release], dev defaults to unwind). The comment explicitly documents that a panic in a handler task unwinds only that task, tokio surfaces it as a JoinError, and "the engine keeps serving other commands. ... Do not re-add panic = abort." So a panic here cannot abort the process.

2) The call site catches it (commands/scan.rs:161-188). load_default runs inside tokio::task::spawn_blocking wrapped in tokio::time::timeout. A panic in spawn_blocking becomes a JoinError, surfaced as the timeout future's Ok(Err(err)) variant — handled at scan.rs:169 in an arm literally commented "model stack load panicked". That arm emits PhaseChanged(Failed) + a model_load_failed error event to the UI, clears scan_state, and returns. The engine process survives and keeps serving IPC.

Reachability of the trigger (FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE>1 + dynamic-batch ONNX + OS thread-spawn failure) is plausible but extremely narrow and opt-in (the file/claim both note this). Even when triggered, the real-world consequence is NOT a process crash: the panic is caught and converted into a clean user-facing "couldn't load models" scan failure; the engine recovers. The only genuine downside vs. the CLIP analog is that a thread-exhaustion at this point fails the whole scan rather than degrading to CLIP-only tagging — a worse UX, but not the crash/abort that defines this bug's claimed harm and "low/robustness" framing. Since the claimed harm (aborts the engine) does not occur, the bug as stated is refuted (benign/recovered). Minor citation errors also noted: the CLIP analog is at pipeline/batch_clip.rs (not "batch_clip.rs" at engine root) and the decoder spawn is tagging.rs:719-730 (claim said 722-730).

contested.title: Rename-heal UPDATE OR REPLACE orphans ocr_fts/doc_fts index entries (FTS5 external-content desync)
contested.description: ocr_fts and doc_fts are FTS5 external-content tables (content='ocr_text'/'doc_text', content_rowid='file_id') with NO sync triggers (grep across the engine finds zero CREATE TRIGGER; the only FTS deletes anywhere are the dbwriter's per-file `DELETE FROM ocr_fts/doc_fts WHERE rowid = file_id` paired writes). The dbwriter keeps the index consistent ONLY for rows it touches by file_id. But the rename-heal statement `UPDATE OR REPLACE files SET path_text=?1 WHERE id=?3` (HEAL_UPDATE_SQL, run at dbwriter.rs:283-285) relocates a healed row onto a path already occupied by another row. SQLite REPLACE then DELETEs that colliding row, and with PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON (db/mod.rs:29) FK-cascades its children (tags, clip_embeddings, text_embeddings, face_prints, ocr_text, doc_text). The colliding row's ocr_text/doc_text rows are cascade-deleted, but its ocr_fts/doc_fts index entries are NOT removed (external-content FTS has no implicit cascade and there are no triggers). Those FTS entries are left orphaned. The subsequent upsert/`ocr_fts_delete` in the same flush only cleans the HEALED row's id, never the REPLACE-victim's id. Result: phantom full-text search hits for the deleted row that linger indefinitely. The HEAL_UPDATE_SQL comment (lines 650-653) enumerates the cascade as 'tags/embeddings/faces' and omits the FTS tables entirely — the author overlooked that the external-content FTS index is neither FK-linked nor trigger-synced. (Same root defect also affects bulk.rs:367 `DELETE FROM files WHERE id=?`, which is out of this subsystem's scope.) files.id is AUTOINCREMENT so the orphaned rowid is never reused by a new file, which bounds severity to wrong/phantom hits rather than misattribution — hence medium, not high.
contested.trigger: User has a copy of a file at path B already cataloged (its OCR/doc text indexed in ocr_fts/doc_fts). Then a DIFFERENT file that shares content identity (file_ref/content_hash) is renamed/moved onto path B with its old path now gone from disk (heal_candidate_moved == true). On the next scan the heal fires `UPDATE OR REPLACE files SET path_text=B`, REPLACE deletes B's old row, B's ocr_text/doc_text cascade away, but B's ocr_fts/doc_fts entries remain. A later search for a term that was only in B's old text returns a hit whose rowid no longer joins to any files row (phantom result / inflated counts). Reproducible by ingesting two such rows then re-scanning with the heal path active.
contested.suggested_fix: Before the heal UPDATE OR REPLACE, explicitly clear the colliding target row's FTS entries inside the same transaction: look up the id at the destination path (`SELECT id FROM files WHERE path_text = ?new`) and, if present and != the healed id, run `DELETE FROM ocr_fts WHERE rowid = ?victim` and `DELETE FROM doc_fts WHERE rowid = ?victim` (while its ocr_text/doc_text rows are still present, so external-content delete tokenizes correctly), THEN perform the heal. Alternatively, replace UPDATE OR REPLACE with an explicit `DELETE FROM files WHERE path_text=?new AND id<>?id` preceded by the FTS deletes, or add AFTER DELETE triggers on ocr_text/doc_text that emit the FTS `'delete'` command so any cascade keeps the index in sync.

verdicts.reasoning:
The finder's STORAGE mechanism is accurate, but the claimed HARM (phantom/wrong search results) does not materialize through the actual query path, so under the logic lens this is benign, not a real correctness bug.

Verified facts:
- ocr_text/doc_text: file_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY REFERENCES files(id) ON DELETE CASCADE (migrations.rs:171-174, 288-291).
- ocr_fts/doc_fts: external-content FTS5 (content='ocr_text'/'doc_text', content_rowid='file_id') (migrations.rs:176-181, 293-298).
- PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON (db/mod.rs:29). files.id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT (migrations.rs:136). files.path_text is UNIQUE ON CONFLICT REPLACE (migrations.rs:137).
- Zero CREATE TRIGGER and zero FTS 'delete' commands anywhere in the engine (grep confirmed). The only FTS deletes are the per-file ocr_fts_delete/doc_fts_delete keyed by the upserted file_id (dbwriter.rs:218-239, 406, 427).
- HEAL_UPDATE_SQL = UPDATE OR REPLACE files SET path_text=?1 WHERE id=?3 (dbwriter.rs:654-658), run at 283-285. HEAL_LOOKUP_SQL excludes the new path (WHERE path_text != ?3, line 641), so a row already at the destination path is a DISTINCT collision victim. REPLACE deletes that victim, FK-cascades its ocr_text/doc_text, and (with no triggers/no implicit external-content cascade) leaves its ocr_fts/doc_fts index entry orphaned. The flush's FTS delete uses the healed/upserted id (RETURNING id), never the victim's id. So the orphan is real at the storage layer, and AUTOINCREMENT means the victim rowid is never reused — the finder got the mechanism right.

Why the asserted harm is refuted:
- The ONLY production consumer of ocr_fts/doc_fts is ReadStore.cs:202-204, which is rooted at `FROM files f ... WHERE f.failed=0 ... AND ( f.id IN (SELECT rowid FROM ocr_fts WHERE ocr_fts MATCH $match) OR f.id IN (SELECT rowid FROM doc_fts WHERE doc_fts MATCH $match) OR ... )`. An orphaned FTS rowid has NO surviving files row, so `f.id IN (...)` can never match it, and AUTOINCREMENT guarantees no future file reuses that id. The orphan is silently filtered out of every query.
- Therefore the finder's stated impact — "phantom full-text search hits that linger indefinitely," "a later search returns a hit whose rowid no longer joins to any files row (phantom result / inflated counts)" — does not occur. The visible result set stays correct; there are no phantom hits and no user-visible inflated counts. No code path anywhere exposes a raw FTS rowid or a bare COUNT(*) over the FTS table to the user (the only such queries are migration unit tests).
- Residual effect is harmless FTS index bloat (a few stale postings) plus a latent FTS5 integrity-check discrepancy that has no consumer in this codebase. That is benign, not a data-correctness defect.

Net: the code does leave an FTS desync (the suggested fix is a reasonable hygiene improvement), but it is not harmful/triggerable as wrong results under the logic lens because the sole search query joins back to files. Confidence is below certainty only because "desynced external-content FTS index" is technically a latent correctness liability if a future consumer ever queries the FTS directly or runs integrity-check/rebuild; today no such consumer exists, so the concrete claimed harm is refuted.

CONFIRMED REAL — every load-bearing element verified in source AND reproduced empirically against the engine's bundled SQLite.

Source verification:
- ocr_fts/doc_fts are FTS5 external-content tables (content='ocr_text'/'doc_text', content_rowid='file_id') — migrations.rs:176-181, 293-298.
- Zero CREATE TRIGGER anywhere (grep across .rs and .sql found none). The FTS index is maintained ONLY by per-file-id `DELETE FROM ocr_fts/doc_fts WHERE rowid=?` in dbwriter.
- files.path_text is `UNIQUE ON CONFLICT REPLACE` (migrations.rs:137); files.id is `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` (migrations.rs:136).
- PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON at every open (db/mod.rs:29).
- ocr_text/doc_text are `REFERENCES files(id) ON DELETE CASCADE` (migrations.rs:172, 289).
- Heal runs `UPDATE OR REPLACE files SET path_text=?1 WHERE id=?3` (dbwriter.rs:283-285; HEAL_UPDATE_SQL:654-658). With UNIQUE ON CONFLICT REPLACE, relocating onto an occupied path DELETEs the colliding (victim) row.
- The flush's ocr_fts_delete/doc_fts_delete key only on the healed row's RETURNING id (dbwriter.rs:405-408, 426-429), never the victim's id.
- The HEAL_UPDATE_SQL comment (lines 652-653) enumerates the cascade as "tags/embeddings/faces" and omits the FTS tables — author overlooked the external-content FTS is neither FK-linked nor trigger-synced, exactly as claimed.

Reachability (the assigned lens): fully satisfiable. HEAL_LOOKUP_SQL (637-648) uses `path_text != ?3`, so it returns the moved file at old path A as a candidate while the victim sits at destination B; heal_candidate_moved (678) fires once A is gone from disk (a genuine rename/move). The content_hash branch covers the cross-volume byte-identical-copy trigger. No upstream guard blocks healing onto an already-occupied path.

Empirical proof: I added a temporary test exercising the real ingest_with_heal + HEAL_UPDATE_SQL + INSERT_FILE_RETURNING_ID_SQL constants against the bundled SQLite. Result: victim row deleted (files_at_B=1, victim_still_row=0), victim ocr_text cascade-deleted (victim_ocr_text=0), but the victim's ocr_fts entry survived — phantom_fts_hits=1 for the victim-only term, phantom_joins_file=0 (the surviving FTS rowid joins to no files row). That is precisely the orphaned/phantom full-text hit described. (Test removed; dbwriter.rs left unmodified — git diff clean.)

Severity medium is correct: files.id AUTOINCREMENT means the orphaned rowid is never reused, bounding impact to phantom/inflated search hits rather than misattribution. Confidence 0.95 (not 1.0) only because the trigger needs a specific real-world sequence — a pre-existing byte-identical copy plus a separate file moved onto its exact path — which is plausible but not the common rename case.

The engine-level mechanics of the claim are accurate, but the claimed HARM (phantom search hits / inflated counts) cannot occur, so under the IMPACT lens this is refuted.

VERIFIED FACTS (all true):
- ocr_fts/doc_fts are external-content FTS5 (content='ocr_text'/'doc_text', content_rowid='file_id'): migrations.rs:176-181, 293-298.
- Zero CREATE TRIGGER in the engine (grep found none); external-content FTS5 has no implicit cascade.
- PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON: db/mod.rs:29. ocr_text/doc_text have REFERENCES files(id) ON DELETE CASCADE: migrations.rs:172, 289.
- path_text is UNIQUE ON CONFLICT REPLACE (migrations.rs:137); HEAL_UPDATE_SQL = UPDATE OR REPLACE files SET path_text=?1 WHERE id=?3 (dbwriter.rs:654-658, executed at 283-285).
- On a heal collision (healed row A relocated onto a path occupied by row B), REPLACE deletes row B, FK-cascades B's ocr_text/doc_text, but leaves B's ocr_fts/doc_fts index entries orphaned. The flush's FTS deletes (dbwriter.rs:405-443) only touch the healed row's id (== A), never B. So the orphaned index entry is genuinely created.

WHY THE HARM DOES NOT MATERIALIZE (the refutation):
The sole production consumer of these FTS tables is ReadStore.cs:197-219 (mirrored on macOS at ReadStore.swift:135): `SELECT f.id, ... FROM files f WHERE f.failed=0 AND ( f.id IN (SELECT rowid FROM ocr_fts WHERE ocr_fts MATCH $match) OR f.id IN (SELECT rowid FROM doc_fts WHERE doc_fts MATCH $match) OR ... )`. The result set is DRIVEN BY the `files` table — a row appears only if its f.id both exists in `files` and is in the FTS rowid set. The orphaned FTS entry has rowid = B, but row B was REPLACE-deleted from `files`, so no `files f` row can match it. The orphan therefore can never surface as a search result. The only direct `SELECT ... FROM ocr_fts` queries that would expose a raw orphan are inside #[cfg(test)] migration unit tests (migrations.rs:398/404/447), never production; nothing counts FTS matches independently.

Additionally, files.id is AUTOINCREMENT (migrations.rs:136), so id B is never reused — the orphan can also never be misattributed to a future legitimate file. Net effect: the orphaned index entry is completely inert dead weight (a tiny, bounded amount of stale index data on disk), not a phantom-hit / inflated-count correctness bug. Under the IMPACT lens the claimed user-visible harm is masked by the join structure and bounded by AUTOINCREMENT. The suggested fix would tidy the index but fixes no observable defect.

Confidence 0.88 (not higher) because the orphaned data does technically exist and a hypothetical future query that selects directly from the FTS table (or uses it as a left/driving table) would surface it; but no such query exists in the current codebase, so the claim as written (phantom hits lingering indefinitely) is not realizable today.

contested.title: OCR never initializes COM/WinRT on its blocking-pool thread; activation fails with CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED and the error is swallowed, silently disabling OCR
contested.description: shell::ocr::recognize performs four WinRT activations on entry — DataWriter::new() (ocr.rs:55), SoftwareBitmap::CreateCopyFromBuffer (ocr.rs:60), OcrEngine::TryCreateFromUserProfileLanguages (ocr.rs:67), and RecognizeAsync(...).get() (ocr.rs:70-74) — but it never initializes COM/WinRT on the calling thread. It is called only via pipeline/tagging.rs:1858-1867 (run_ocr_blocking) on a tokio spawn_blocking thread, and main.rs performs no global CoInitialize/RoInitialize (verified: no CoInitialize/RoInitialize anywhere in main.rs). Every other shell module deliberately initializes COM per its contract — trash.rs:50 and tags.rs:185 call CoInitializeEx, thumbnail.rs:56, reveal.rs:36, and video.rs:48-57 (ensure_com_initialized) do the same — and video.rs:36-47 documents exactly this hazard: 'COM must be initialized on every thread that drives [WinRT]. The decoder pool spawns raw OS threads and Deep Analyze runs ... on tokio blocking-pool threads — neither inherits the COM apartment ...'. windows-rs 0.58 WinRT activation (RoGetActivationFactory) does NOT auto-initialize the apartment, so on any blocking thread that has not previously run a COM-initializing shell op (e.g. a fresh thread, or one that only ever ran OCR), DataWriter::new()/TryCreateFromUserProfileLanguages return CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED (0x800401F0) / RO_E_UNINITIALIZED. run_ocr_blocking maps every Err to Ok(None) (tagging.rs:1861-1864, 'OCR skipped'), so the failure is invisible: screenshots and scanned documents silently receive no OCR text tags. Whether OCR works at all becomes dependent on tokio thread-reuse history (a thread that previously ran video::keyframe_25pct leaves an MTA behind and OCR happens to work), making the bug intermittent across runs.
contested.trigger: Scan a folder containing a screenshot/scanned image so the tagging pipeline calls run_ocr_blocking on a tokio blocking thread that has not previously executed video/trash/tags COM init. DataWriter::new() returns CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED; run_ocr_blocking swallows it to Ok(None); the image gets no OCR-derived tags even though Windows.Media.Ocr is installed and functional.
contested.suggested_fix: Mirror video.rs's per-thread guard: add a thread-local one-shot ensure_com_initialized() (CoInitializeEx(None, COINIT_MULTITHREADED), RPC_E_CHANGED_MODE tolerated) and call it at the top of recognize() before the first WinRT call. MTA matches the other blocking-pool init and needs no message pump.

verdicts.reasoning:
Independently verified every load-bearing claim by reading the source.

1) ocr.rs:34-74 — recognize() performs four WinRT activations (DataWriter::new() L55, SoftwareBitmap::CreateCopyFromBuffer L60, OcrEngine::TryCreateFromUserProfileLanguages L67, RecognizeAsync().get() L70-74) and contains NO CoInitialize/RoInitialize/ensure_com_initialized in the function or module.

2) main.rs:69 builds a new_multi_thread runtime; grep for CoInitialize/RoInitialize in main.rs returns nothing. A repo-wide grep shows COM init exists ONLY in the 5 shell modules (trash.rs:50/108, tags.rs:185, thumbnail.rs:56, reveal.rs:36, video.rs:48-57) — never process-wide, never in ocr.rs.

3) tagging.rs:1857-1868 — run_ocr_blocking executes OCR inside tokio::task::spawn_blocking (tokio's dedicated blocking thread pool) and maps every Err(...) to Ok(None) with only tracing::debug!("OCR skipped"). Call site tagging.rs:1674 (`if let Ok(Some(ocr))`) further discards the distinction. The failure is invisible to the user.

4) The benevolent thread-reuse path the finding hypothesizes does not even exist within the tagging pipeline: video keyframe decode (which DOES call ensure_com_initialized) runs on named RAW OS threads `fileid-decode-{idx}` (tagging.rs:715-721 via std::thread::Builder), NOT on the tokio blocking pool. So the OCR blocking-pool thread inherits no COM apartment from tagging's own decode stage. (commands/thumbnail.rs:40 and deep_analyze.rs:460 do run video on the blocking pool and can leave an MTA behind on a reused blocking thread — which is exactly the intermittency the finding predicts.)

5) video.rs:36-47 explicitly documents this hazard ("COM must be initialized on every thread that drives [WinRT]... tokio blocking-pool threads... neither inherits the COM apartment... fails with CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED"), and states it "mirrors the per-thread CoInitializeEx the other shell::* modules already do" — establishing that ocr.rs omitting it is a real inconsistency, not an intentional design choice.

6) windows = "0.58" (Cargo.toml:153). windows-rs WinRT activation (RoGetActivationFactory) does not implicitly initialize the apartment in this version; an uninitialized thread returns CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED / RO_E_UNINITIALIZED for in-proc factory activation. DataWriter/SoftwareBitmap/OcrEngine all activate through this path.

Conclusion: genuine, reachable on a fresh/OCR-only tokio blocking thread, and harmful — screenshots/scanned docs silently receive no OCR text tags, with behavior dependent on thread-reuse history (intermittent). The suggested fix (a thread-local one-shot ensure_com_initialized mirroring video.rs, MTA, tolerating RPC_E_CHANGED_MODE) is correct.

Confidence held below ~0.9 only because the precise CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED-vs-success outcome of a specific WinRT activation on an uninitialized thread is the one piece I verified from windows-rs/COM semantics rather than by executing on the owner-gated runtime/GPU path; the code-logic gap itself is unambiguous and matches a hazard the codebase already documents and guards everywhere except here.

The bug's load-bearing technical premise is FALSE for windows-rs 0.58. The claim asserts "windows-rs 0.58 WinRT activation (RoGetActivationFactory) does NOT auto-initialize the apartment," so DataWriter::new() etc. return CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED on a fresh blocking thread. I read the actual compiled crate source and it does the opposite.

Decisive evidence — windows-core-0.58.0/src/imp/factory_cache.rs:87-95 (the `factory::<C,I>()` function every WinRT activation funnels through):
```
let mut code = get_com_factory();           // first RoGetActivationFactory
if code == CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED {
    let mut cookie = core::ptr::null_mut();
    CoIncrementMTAUsage(&mut cookie);        // create implicit process MTA + join calling thread
    code = get_com_factory();                // retry — now succeeds
}
```
The comment at lines 87-88 states the intent verbatim: "If RoGetActivationFactory fails because combase hasn't been loaded yet then load combase automatically so that it 'just works' for apartment-agnostic code." CoIncrementMTAUsage is unconditionally linked (imp/bindings.rs:17, ole32.dll) and called (factory_cache.rs:91) with no cfg-gating — verified it is the real compiled path.

Trigger-path / reachability analysis (my lens):
- Confirmed ocr.rs:55 DataWriter::new() is the FIRST WinRT call in recognize(); it routes through FactoryCache::call → factory() (windows-0.58.0 Storage/Streams/mod.rs:1336-1342, `Self::IActivationFactory` → `FactoryCache::new()/.call`).
- SoftwareBitmap::CreateCopyFromBuffer (Imaging/mod.rs:1891 → ISoftwareBitmapStatics → FactoryCache) and OcrEngine::TryCreateFromUserProfileLanguages (Media/Ocr/mod.rs:133-143 → IOcrEngineStatics → FactoryCache) take the same auto-init path.
