- So on a fresh tokio blocking thread with NO apartment, DataWriter::new() hits CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED internally, calls CoIncrementMTAUsage (creating the implicit MTA and making the thread an implicit-MTA member), retries, and SUCCEEDS. The thread is now MTA for the remaining calls, so SoftwareBitmap/OcrEngine succeed and RecognizeAsync().get() (an instance call + IAsyncOperation wait, which needs no STA pump on an MTA thread) works.
- Verified main.rs (lines 44-79) does no CoInitialize/RoInitialize and only builds a multi_thread tokio runtime; verified run_ocr_blocking (tagging.rs:1857-1868) swallows Err→Ok(None). Both of those facts in the report are TRUE — but they are harmless because the activation never actually fails on the count claimed.

Conclusion: the cited failure (DataWriter::new() returns CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED, swallowed to Ok(None), OCR silently disabled, intermittent per thread-reuse) does not occur. Activation self-heals on first use via CoIncrementMTAUsage; OCR is deterministic regardless of thread history. video.rs needs its explicit ensure_com_initialized() only because Media Foundation's MFCreateSourceReaderFromURL is a classic-COM call that does NOT have the windows-rs WinRT auto-init wrapper — that asymmetry is exactly why OCR (pure WinRT/RoGetActivationFactory) legitimately doesn't need one. The suggested fix would be harmless but is unnecessary. Residual ~8% uncertainty: I could not exercise the runtime on the owner's hardware to observe a live OCR activation, but the source-level evidence is unambiguous.

The bug's load-bearing technical premise is factually false. The claim asserts windows-rs 0.58 WinRT activation does NOT auto-initialize the apartment, so DataWriter::new()/TryCreateFromUserProfileLanguages return CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED on an uninitialized blocking thread and the error is swallowed, silently disabling OCR. Reading the actual crate source disproves this.

windows-core-0.58.0/src/imp/factory_cache.rs lines 73-117 define factory(), the function backing every WinRT activation. Lines 87-95 explicitly handle CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED: `if code == CO_E_NOTINITIALIZED { CoIncrementMTAUsage(&mut cookie); code = get_com_factory(); }` with the comment "load combase automatically so that it 'just works' for apartment-agnostic code." CoIncrementMTAUsage joins the calling thread to the process implicit MTA, and the cookie is leaked (process-lifetime), so the thread stays in the MTA. The factory call then succeeds on retry.

I confirmed all four WinRT entry points in ocr.rs route through this path: DataWriter::new() -> FactoryCache::call (windows-0.58.0 Storage/Streams/mod.rs:1336-1342); SoftwareBitmap::CreateCopyFromBuffer -> FactoryCache::call (Graphics/Imaging/mod.rs:1887-1895); OcrEngine::TryCreateFromUserProfileLanguages -> FactoryCache::call (Media/Ocr/mod.rs:133-143). RecognizeAsync(...).get() is a vtable method call on an already-activated agile object and works on an MTA thread without a message pump. So the very first activation (DataWriter::new() at ocr.rs:55) bootstraps the MTA and every subsequent call succeeds, regardless of tokio thread-reuse history.

The peripheral facts the claim cites are accurate: main.rs has no CoInitialize/RoInitialize (Grep: 0 matches); other shell modules do per-thread COM init (reveal.rs:36, video.rs:48-52, thumbnail.rs:56, trash.rs:50/108, tags.rs:185); run_ocr_blocking swallows Err to Ok(None) (tagging.rs:1859-1864); the OCR path is reachable for FileKind::Image (tagging.rs:1668-1674) on a default tokio blocking thread (main.rs:69-71, no on_thread_start apartment hook). But none of that produces the claimed harm because the WinRT factory self-initializes the MTA. video.rs genuinely needs its manual ensure_com_initialized because MFCreateSourceReaderFromURL is classic COM (CoCreateInstance-style), not a windows-rs WinRT activatable, and therefore does NOT benefit from the factory_cache auto-init — so video.rs's pattern does not imply OCR needs it. Under the IMPACT lens: OCR is not silently disabled, the failure is not intermittent, and the suggested fix would be a harmless no-op rather than a correctness fix. Refuted. Confidence below 1.0 only because runtime-on-hardware behavior is owner-gated and I could not execute it, but the source path is unambiguous.

contested.title: revertMerge sets the destination person's file_count to the SOURCE person's count
contested.description:
The single recompute UPDATE is:
    UPDATE persons SET file_count = (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT file_id) FROM face_prints WHERE person_id = ?1) WHERE id IN (?1, ?2)
with ?1 = new_pid (the restored source person) and ?2 = payload.destination_person_id. The correlated subquery always counts person_id = ?1 (the source), but the WHERE clause writes that one value to BOTH the source row AND the destination row. After a revert, the destination person's file_count is overwritten with the source person's face-file count instead of being recomputed from the destination's own remaining faces. The destination People card then shows a wrong file count. This is inconsistent with handle_merge_clusters (bulk.rs:463-466), which correctly recomputes only the destination using its own id. Trigger: merge person A into B, then call revertMerge to split A back out — B's file_count card is now wrong (equal to A's count) until the next full re-clustering pass recomputes both.
contested.trigger: Merge cluster #A into #B (mergeClusters), then RevertMerge with source_person_id=A, destination_person_id=B and a non-empty face_ids_to_revert: persons.file_count for B is set to A's restored count instead of B's actual remaining count; the People card displays the wrong number.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual source at trash.rs:219-223 (handle_revert_merge) and the reference at bulk.rs:463-466 (handle_merge_clusters).

The buggy statement is:
  UPDATE persons SET file_count = (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT file_id) FROM face_prints WHERE person_id = ?1) WHERE id IN (?1, ?2)
bound with ?1 = new_pid (restored source) and ?2 = payload.destination_person_id (trash.rs:222).

Re-derived independently: in SQLite, ?1 is a numbered BIND PARAMETER, not a correlated reference to the outer persons.id column. It is bound once to new_pid for the whole statement. The UPDATE matches two rows (id = new_pid and id = destination_person_id), and for BOTH rows the SET subquery evaluates COUNT(... WHERE person_id = new_pid). So the destination (B) row's file_count is overwritten with the SOURCE (A) person's face-file count instead of being recomputed from B's own remaining faces. This is the bug as claimed.

Contrast with the correct reference: handle_merge_clusters (bulk.rs:464) uses "...WHERE person_id = ?1 ... WHERE id = ?1" binding the SAME dst id to both, so the subquery and the WHERE refer to the same person — self-consistent. The revert path instead binds two different ids and keys the count only on the first.

Reachability: handler is dispatched from main.rs:768 (CommandPayload::RevertMerge -> handle_revert_merge) and exposed to the app via EngineClient.RevertMergeAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:753). Payload fields are distinct (sourcePersonID/destinationPersonID/faceIDsToRevert per ipc/mod.rs:388-395). In the normal split-back flow new_pid != destination_person_id, so the wrong value is written to B's card. It only coincidentally produces the right value in the degenerate self-revert case (ids equal, IN collapses to one row).

Harm: a visible People-card file_count error (cached denorm), not face-data corruption; self-heals on next full re-clustering. Medium severity is fair. The finder's only imprecision is calling the subquery "correlated" — it is actually a plain bind-parameter subquery — but the conclusion (always counts the source) and consequence are exactly right. The suggested fix (two per-id statements, or key the subquery on persons.id) is correct. Confidence 0.92, dropped slightly only because no live UI caller path was located (command method exists but the People UI invocation wasn't found), though IPC reachability alone makes it triggerable.

The SQL defect itself is mechanically real. At trash.rs:219-223 the statement is `UPDATE persons SET file_count = (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT file_id) FROM face_prints WHERE person_id = ?1) WHERE id IN (?1, ?2)` with ?1 = new_pid (restored source) and ?2 = payload.destination_person_id. The correlated subquery keys on person_id = ?1 (source) and is constant across the two outer rows, while WHERE id IN (?1,?2) writes that single value to BOTH rows. So the destination's file_count is overwritten with the source's face-file count rather than recomputed from its own remaining faces. This genuinely diverges from the correct pattern in handle_merge_clusters (bulk.rs:463-466), which uses subquery key dst and scope `WHERE id = ?1` (=dst) only. So the description's logic is accurate.

However, under my assigned TRIGGER/REACHABILITY lens, the harmful path is NOT reachable with real inputs in the shipping product. The command IS wired at the engine/IPC boundary (ipc.schema.json:246, ipc/mod.rs:161/388, dispatch at main.rs:768, handler handle_revert_merge), and the C# DTO/serializer exist (CommandPayload.cs:133/193/251). But the ONLY producer of a RevertMergeCommand is EngineClient.RevertMergeAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:753-754), and that wrapper has ZERO callers — I searched the entire platforms/windows tree (.cs/.xaml incl. tests) and found no button, command binding, menu, or any UI surface that invokes RevertMergeAsync. The merge UI (SuggestedMergesSheet.xaml.cs) calls MergeClustersAsync and MarkPersonsAsDifferentAsync but never RevertMerge. Thus no user action emits a revertMerge command, so the wrong-file_count outcome cannot manifest in production today.

The bug would become real and harmful the moment a UI revert-merge action is added (the feature is half-plumbed). But per the strict reachability lens and the refute-by-default mandate, the cited runtime path is currently untriggerable by real inputs, so I refute. Confidence is moderate (not high) because the engine-side path is fully live and a future/test/direct-IPC caller would trip the genuine defect — this is "real defect, currently unreachable in the shipping UI" rather than "not a bug."

The SQL-divergence half of the claim is accurate, but the asserted HARM (wrong People-card count) does not occur, so under the IMPACT lens the bug is refuted as benign.

SQL logic (accurate): At platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/trash.rs:219-223 the statement is `UPDATE persons SET file_count = (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT file_id) FROM face_prints WHERE person_id = ?1) WHERE id IN (?1, ?2)` with params [new_pid, destination_person_id]. In SQLite, `?1` is a positional BOUND parameter (a literal value = new_pid), not a correlated reference to the outer persons.id. So the subquery evaluates to one constant (the source's count) and is written to BOTH the source row and the destination row matched by `WHERE id IN (?1, ?2)`. This does diverge from the correct per-destination recompute at bulk.rs:464 (`...WHERE id = ?1`, keyed on the destination's own id). So the stored destination persons.file_count is genuinely overwritten with the source's value after a revertMerge.

IMPACT (refuted): The claimed consequence — "the destination People card shows a wrong file count" — does not happen, because persons.file_count is never read for any user-visible count. I grep-verified every reader of the column across engine + app: the only occurrences are the WRITES (trash.rs:204/220, bulk.rs:464, face_clustering.rs:341 insert, migrations.rs:187 schema). There is no SELECT of p.file_count anywhere. The actual count sources all recompute live from face_prints:
- People tab card (PeopleViewModel.cs:173): `COUNT(fp.id)` via JOIN face_prints, GROUP BY p.id — does NOT use file_count.
- Merge-suggestions sheet counts surfaced as Source/DestinationMemberCount (bulk.rs:701 `COUNT(fpc.id)`, emitted bulk.rs:857-858) — computed live.
- find_merge_suggestions and restructure plan — also live / no file_count read.

The face_prints reassignment in handle_revert_merge (trash.rs:212-217) is correct and is the real source of truth; the wrong number lands only in an unread, vestigial denormalized column and gets rewritten on the next clustering pass anyway. No crash, hang, data loss, corruption of user-visible state, leak, or telemetry. The People card recomputes correctly on every load, so the user never sees a wrong count.

Caveat lowering confidence from certainty: the fix is still a legitimately correct cleanup (the divergence from bulk.rs is real and worth fixing), and I could not run a live SQLite session to empirically confirm the ?1-is-literal semantics (no sqlite3 CLI in env) — but that parameter-binding behavior is unambiguous and standard. Even granting the SQL is wrong, the specific stated harm is not triggerable, so the claim is refuted on impact.

contested.title: Panic in the inline (non-spawned) stdio dispatch arms hangs the engine instead of exiting
contested.description: The stdio reader runs as a detached `tokio::spawn` (stdio_loop, line 329) and the main task awaits ONLY the `main_shutdown` Notify (line 396); it never awaits the stdio_loop JoinHandle and never observes its result. Per-command work that is `tokio::spawn`'d is panic-isolated, but several dispatch arms run INLINE on the stdio_loop task itself (PauseScan/ResumeScan/CancelScan at 561-578 call coord methods under scan_state.lock(); DeepAnalyzeCancel 753; CancelPrewarm 522; Shutdown 505; plus the inline sink .await sends). If any of those inline paths panics, the panic hook logs it and the stdio_loop task terminates — but no `shutdown.notify_waiters()` is ever fired, so the main task remains blocked on `main_shutdown.await` forever, the WAL checkpoint/teardown never runs, and the engine stops reading stdin while the process stays alive. The parent-PID watchdog only rescues this if the parent app actually dies; while the WinUI app is alive the engine is a permanent zombie that silently ignores every subsequent command. The clear design intent (spawn-per-command for panic isolation) is defeated by the inline arms having no panic boundary and by main not treating stdio_loop completion as a shutdown trigger.
contested.trigger: A panic on the stdio_loop task while executing an inline dispatch arm (e.g. a coordinator method invoked from PauseScan/CancelScan, or any future inline arm that indexes/expects) terminates the reader task without signaling shutdown; the engine then hangs indefinitely (parent alive), unresponsive to all IPC.
contested.suggested_fix: Make stdio_loop completion drive shutdown so a dead reader can't strand the process: either select! the stdio_loop JoinHandle alongside main_shutdown (`tokio::select! { _ = &mut main_shutdown => {}, _ = &mut stdio_loop => { /* reader gone: proceed to teardown */ } }`), or wrap the inline dispatch body so a panic in the reader fires `shutdown.notify_waiters()` before unwinding. This restores the per-command panic isolation the spawn-based arms already have.

verdicts.reasoning:
I re-derived the lifecycle from source and the finder's logic holds.

Verified facts:
- Cargo.toml:239-245 sets `panic = "unwind"` ON PURPOSE (documented B7 in DECISIONS.md:100): a task panic unwinds ONLY that task and the process survives. This is exactly the precondition for a zombie — under "abort" the whole process would die instead, and no hang could occur.
- main.rs:329 spawns the stdio reader as a detached `tokio::spawn` (stdio_loop). handle_line and several dispatch arms execute INLINE on that task: handle_line itself (354), Shutdown (505-508), CancelPrewarm (522-526), PauseScan/ResumeScan/CancelScan (561-578), DeepAnalyzeCancel (753-756), plus inline sink .await sends.
- main.rs:396 the main task awaits ONLY `main_shutdown.await`. It never selects on, nor inspects, the stdio_loop JoinHandle. The `stdio_loop.abort()` at 420 runs only AFTER shutdown is already signaled, and the JoinError (panic) result is never observed (422 awaits sink_writer, not stdio_loop).
- Every NON-panic loop exit fires `dispatch_shutdown.notify_waiters()` (EOF 381, read-err 386); a panic exit fires nothing. So a panic on the reader task strands main on main_shutdown.await indefinitely.
- platform.rs:743-789 watch_parent only signals shutdown when the parent process actually exits; while the WinUI app is alive it cannot rescue the zombie. Confirms "permanent zombie, no WAL checkpoint, all subsequent IPC silently ignored."
- The eng-ipc-0/PAR-111 JoinError->terminal-event fix (NEXT.md:145, DECISIONS.md:2803) is implemented INSIDE the spawned handlers (bulk.rs:496, embed.rs:93/202, face_clustering.rs:399, restructure.rs:102/305) — it does NOT cover the stdio_loop task, so this gap is genuinely unguarded.
- B7's own wording ("the stdio loop dispatches every IPC handler on a bare tokio::spawn") shows the team's mental model assumes ALL handlers are spawned; the inline arms violate that assumption, so the documented panic-isolation intent is genuinely defeated for them.

The fix is correct and low-risk (select! the stdio_loop JoinHandle alongside main_shutdown, or notify_waiters on reader unwind).

Counterweight (why not high confidence): I could not find a concrete panic that is reachable on the inline path TODAY. The cited inline arms are all panic-free as written — atomic stores, non-poisoning parking_lot locks, notify_waiters, and Sink::send swallows its error (sink.rs:56-61). serde decode is Result-handled (486-495). No unwrap/index/expect on any inline path. So the trigger is latent/defensive (a future inline arm, or a runtime/serde panic) rather than demonstrable from a specific input now. The defect is a real, correctly-described latent lifecycle hazard that defeats the documented design intent and would be severe if hit, but its present-day triggerability is unproven — hence "real" at moderate confidence rather than high.

The lifecycle MECHANISM the claim describes is structurally accurate, but under the assigned TRIGGER/REACHABILITY lens the bug is not triggerable, so I refute it.

Mechanism (verified): `stdio_loop` is a detached `tokio::spawn` (main.rs:329) that is never awaited; main awaits only the `main_shutdown` Notify (main.rs:396); `stdio_loop.abort()` (main.rs:420) runs only after that await returns. `Cargo.toml:239-245` confirms `panic = "unwind"` (explicitly, intentionally — comment B7), so a panic inside `handle_line` would unwind the stdio_loop future and be captured by tokio as a JoinError; the panic hook (logging.rs:57-77) only logs, it does not abort. A panic in a NON-spawned (inline) arm would therefore kill the reader task without firing `shutdown.notify_waiters()`, stranding main on line 396 (while the parent is alive) and skipping the WAL checkpoint. That much is real.

Reachability (the lens) — the trigger is NOT satisfiable today. I read every inline arm and they all do strictly infallible work:
- Shutdown (505): notify_waiters() — and it IS the shutdown signal.
- CancelPrewarm (522): cancel_prewarm → HashMap entry + atomic store + tracing (prewarm.rs:76-91). Panic-free.
- PauseScan/ResumeScan/CancelScan (561-578): parking_lot Mutex lock (never poisons/panics) + coordinator methods that are pure AtomicBool stores + notify_waiters (coordinator.rs:74-87). Panic-free.
- DeepAnalyzeCancel (753): atomic store + tracing. Panic-free.
- Inline sink sends (emit_db_unavailable 829, oversized-frame 370): Sink::send swallows send errors (sink.rs:56-61); the only fallible step (serde serialization) runs on the SEPARATE writer task (sink.rs:33-49), already panic-isolated — not on the stdio_loop task.

A grep of main.rs for unwrap()/expect()/indexing/panic!/unreachable! returns ZERO matches. The inline arms cannot panic with any real IPC input; the IPC contract is already deserialized and validated before dispatch, and the inline handlers touch no data that could index/unwrap out of bounds.

The claim itself concedes this by hedging "or any future inline arm that indexes/expects" — i.e., it describes a latent fragility / defense-in-depth gap, not a presently-reachable defect. The suggested fix (select! on the JoinHandle, or a panic boundary around the inline body) is reasonable hardening, but no real input reaches a panic on the reader task as the code stands. Under the strict trigger/reachability lens, this is not a real, triggerable bug. Confidence held below 0.9 because the structural risk is genuine and any future inline arm doing fallible work would convert this into a live bug.

The structural mechanism the claim describes is verified-correct. I confirmed: (1) the stdio reader is a detached `tokio::spawn` (main.rs:329) whose JoinHandle (`stdio_loop`) is only used at line 420 for `.abort()` during teardown — it is never awaited or `select!`'d; (2) the main task parks on `main_shutdown.await` at line 396, which awaits only the `shutdown` Notify, not stdio_loop completion; (3) panic strategy is `unwind`, set deliberately in Cargo.toml:234-245 with a comment that the per-command `tokio::spawn` arms are panic-isolated; (4) the panic hook (logging.rs:57-77) only logs and leaves unwinding intact; (5) there is no `catch_unwind`/`AssertUnwindSafe` anywhere in main.rs. The inline dispatch arms (Shutdown 505, CancelPrewarm 522, PauseScan 561, ResumeScan 567, CancelScan 573, DeepAnalyzeCancel 753, RequestStatus 498, VerifyCudaPack 502) genuinely run on the stdio_loop task itself with no spawn boundary. So IF one of them panicked, the panic would unwind the stdio_loop task, its JoinError would be unobserved, `shutdown.notify_waiters()` would never fire, and the main task would hang forever — WAL checkpoint (402-415) never runs, engine stops reading stdin while the process stays alive (parent watchdog at 264-271 only rescues if the parent actually dies). The diagnosis of the asymmetry and the suggested fix (select! on the JoinHandle, or fire notify_waiters before unwinding) are both correct and sound.

The reason I cannot return "real" is the IMPACT/triggerability lens: NO current inline arm can actually panic. I read the inline code paths: the coordinator methods called by PauseScan/ResumeScan/CancelScan (coordinator.rs:74-87) are pure `AtomicBool::store` + `Notify::notify_waiters()`; DeepAnalyzeCancel is an atomic store + tracing; Shutdown is notify_waiters + tracing; CancelPrewarm is a registry lookup; `scan_state.lock()` uses parking_lot (no poisoning, no panic); the sink-send arms go through `Sink::send` which swallows send errors silently (sink.rs:56-61) and never panics; serde decode failure is already guarded with an early return (main.rs:486-495). There is no `unwrap()`/`expect()`/slice-index/arithmetic on hostile input in any current inline arm. The claim's own trigger language hedges on this ("...or any future inline arm that indexes/expects"). So the defect is a genuine, correctly-described latent robustness/architecture gap with a valid fix, but it is essentially un-triggerable in the code as it stands today — the claimed hang does not occur in practice with the current inline arms. That places it between "real" (mechanism + fix are right) and "refuted" (not presently reachable/harmful), hence uncertain. It is a legitimate defensive hardening, not a presently-manifesting bug.
